[[Anu Hariharan]] and Nic Dardenne analyzed Pinduoduo as a case study in how social commerce can create entirely new market categories rather than competing head-on with established transactional platforms.

## The Core Insight: Shopping Is Social

Pinduoduo found space in e-commerce not as a competitor to search-based platforms like JD, but as a new category: interactive and social shopping [1]. The fundamental observation is that in the physical world, "shopping is meant to be 'interactive and fun' and purchases are regularly informed by friends and family" [1]. Traditional e-commerce platforms like JD, Alibaba, and Amazon optimize for efficiency, funnel conversion, and purchase rates. They do not account for the social dimension of shopping.

The evidence for this gap was visible in category penetration data: in 2020 in the US, only 29% of apparel, 11% of health and personal care, and 3% of food and beverage purchases happened online, compared to 55% of books/music and 43% of computers/electronics [1]. Social shopping is particularly important for categories where consumers seek feedback or recommendations from friends.

## Team Purchase: The Core Mechanic

The core of Pinduoduo's experience is team purchase, where buyers form groups to receive discounts from suppliers [1]. The flow works as follows:

1. Merchants set two prices for each item: individual purchase and team purchase [1]
2. A user opting for team purchase can either initiate a new team or join an existing one [1]
3. The initiator shares via [[WeChat]] to recruit friends, or waits for other Pinduoduo users to join [1]
4. The team must form within 24 hours for the order to be confirmed [1]
5. Once formed, the product ships within 48 hours [1]

![Pinduoduo Team Purchase Flow](images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/22cf244a2ed3.jpg)

Nearly all Pinduoduo transactions use team purchase [1]. In early days, groups were large (10+ members), but requirements have declined as the platform scaled [1].

### Why Team Purchase Is Not Groupon

The comparison to Groupon is common but misleading [1]:

| Dimension | Pinduoduo | Groupon |
|---|---|---|
| Deal design | By sellers, initiated by consumers | Designed and managed by sellers |
| Product type | Everyday goods (fruits, vegetables, apparel) | One-off products/experiences |
| Merchant geography | Diverse merchants across China and globally | Mostly local sellers |
| Consumer value | Discounts on goods users need anyway | Aggregating demand on low-value/excess inventory |

### Strategic Launch Category

Pinduoduo launched in fruits and vegetables for two strategic reasons [1]:
1. Incumbents were focused on non-perishable items, limiting competition
2. Fruits and vegetables are low order value, high frequency goods, creating habitual usage

When buyers saw good deals, they forwarded them to neighbors and friends via [[WeChat]] to form teams of 10+ buyers. Group initiators received produce for free for helping Pinduoduo acquire 10 incremental users [1]. This drove organic sharing and rapid user growth: only one month after launching their first-party app in January 2016, Pinduoduo had over 10 million customers [1].

For comparison, Alibaba crossed the 500 million active buyer threshold 14-15 years after launch versus just 4 years for Pinduoduo [1].

### Network Effects

Pinduoduo exhibits what Hariharan calls one of the most powerful network effects in the world, governed by [Reed's law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reed%27s_law): "The value of a group-forming network is proportional to number and the ease with which groups form within it" [1]. The comparison is to Slack and WhatsApp Groups, which grew exponentially by tapping into groups of social relationships.

The key takeaway for startups: "New commerce platforms that can leverage social relationships will accelerate the offline to online transition and if you are able to tap into groups of social relationships vs. one-to-one relationship the faster your platforms will scale" [1].

## WeChat as Growth Platform

A significant enabler of Pinduoduo's virality was the widespread use of [[WeChat]] in China. Tencent (WeChat's owner) is a large investor in Pinduoduo and allowed the platform to grow on top of the WeChat ecosystem [1]. Hariharan notes that "it is highly unlikely that Facebook would allow a social commerce application like Pinduoduo to be built on top of Messenger or Instagram in the US" but adds that messaging fragmentation in the US means this is not necessarily a blocker for US-based social e-commerce [1].

## Gamification and Engagement

Pinduoduo operates on the principle that users visit without specific purchase intent, "much like visiting a real-world shopping mall" [1]. Time spent directly correlates with purchases, so Pinduoduo invested heavily in gamified engagement features.

### Daily Check-Ins

Users receive redeemable points for daily check-ins. Rewards accumulate over many months, and users must reach a minimum value (e.g., 30 RMB) to cash out as a no-minimum-spend voucher [1]. This creates a simple daily habit loop that eventually ties back to commerce.

![Pinduoduo Daily Check-In](images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/e621e756cab8.png)

### Price Chop

Users can get products for free by sharing a custom link with friends within a 24-hour window [1]. Each friend who clicks and engages with Pinduoduo (no purchase required) drives an incremental discount for the initiator. The difficulty mechanics mirror MMORPG leveling: "it becomes harder to level up as you graduate to higher levels" [1]. Difficulty is customized based on the user (easier for low-engagement users) and the item (expensive items are harder to chop) [1].

Price Chop simultaneously increases customer lifetime value and acquires new customers through user-to-user sharing [1].

![Pinduoduo Price Chop](images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/bf7bb9afff8b.png)

### Card Programs

Three key card types drive specific user behaviors [1]:

1. **Free Pass Card**: Loyalty-style card allowing team purchase discounts without joining a team. Earned after two purchases.
2. **Brand Black Card**: Encourages reviews by offering discounts on branded products after 2-4 reviews. Improves platform quality for all users.
3. **Brand Card**: Promotes branded products. Users share brand cards with friends via WeChat, leveraging trusted friend recommendations to drive branded product adoption.

### Mini Games

Pinduoduo hosts in-app games to drive daily engagement. **Duo Duo Orchard** (described as "Farmville except now the rewards are real physical goods") has users nurture a virtual fruit tree that eventually yields a real box of fruit shipped to their address [1]. The game had more than 11 million DAUs. Progress is tied to behaviors that drive revenue: shopping on Pinduoduo yields water droplets for your tree, and team purchases unlock special tools [1].

**DD Bank** is a piggy bank game where users accumulate exchangeable coins over time [1].

The games create "a win-win-win situation for merchants, consumers, and themselves. More specifically, merchants see more volume, consumers have fun and receive unique discounts, and Pinduoduo generates revenue" [1].

## Personalized Recommendations

Unlike search-based platforms, Pinduoduo is recommendation-driven [1]. Based on a user's social graph, category preferences, and trust relationships, the platform personalizes the experience. For new customers, Pinduoduo shows different categories based on what they know about the user and their friends, then uses interactions to build a buying persona [1].

Personalization extends to value perception: based on purchase and browsing history, Pinduoduo determines whether a user values high-cost branded or low-cost non-branded items and adjusts recommendations to match willingness to pay [1].

## Implications for Global E-Commerce

Hariharan argues that while team purchase may be specific to China's commercial landscape, the underlying insight about social integration in commerce is "likely entirely universal" [1]. The core takeaway: "Web 1.0 platforms -- including Amazon -- optimize for efficiency and don't serve trust-based categories very well" [1].

YC portfolio companies already pursuing variations of this model [1]:
- **[Snackpass](https://www.snackpass.co/)**: A food app for college campuses with social features (gift-sending, virtual pet hatching through food orders)
- **[Meesho](https://meesho.com/)**: An Indian reseller marketplace where customers create microbusinesses selling to friends and family via WhatsApp

"Social commerce does not just mean connecting user accounts to Facebook. It means investing in creating physical world experiences online -- specifically bringing the fun of shopping offline to online platforms" [1].

## References

1. [Pinduoduo and the Rise of Social E-Commerce](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce) — [[Anu Hariharan]], Nic Dardenne

title: Snapdocs: Navigating Market Cycles
body:
# Snapdocs: Navigating Market Cycles

[Snapdocs](https://www.snapdocs.com/) is the leading digital closing platform for the mortgage industry. As of the interview, the company touches 25% of all US real estate transactions and is valued at $1.5B. Founder and CEO [[Aaron King]] (YC W14) built the company with minimal outside funding through volatile market conditions [1].

## Capital Efficiency as a Discipline

Snapdocs started as a side project years before [[Aaron King]] applied to YC. By the time he applied, the company had a live product, customers, and revenue. Even after YC, the company did not raise much immediately [1].

Three years after YC, Snapdocs raised its Series A. At that point, the company had spent about $1M of its seed round and was at a $5M revenue run rate. The lead investor later told King how capital efficient Snapdocs had been compared to peers [1].

King frames capital discipline not as a constraint but as a virtue: "I've learned that you should take the same approach even when you do have the capital to be less disciplined" [1]. Capital constraints kept the team focused on going deep with paying customers, avoiding the common trap of building products no one wanted [1].

The approach also forced the team to think about the smallest version of every product in order to ship quickly, maintaining a short feedback loop with users [1]. King acknowledges the cost: "We built many things that weren't engineered for scale or flexibility. However, now we can afford to reengineer those unscalable solutions because we built something people want" [1].

## Product Development

Before raising the Series A, Snapdocs was heavy on customer involvement. The team spent time in customers' offices watching them use the product and understanding their work [1]. They also kept prospects in the loop as they built new features:

> "Some of the best feedback came from people who had chosen to not yet work with us. Responding to that feedback with a killer feature was a great way to ultimately get them on board." [1]

The in-person interactions built trust and rapport. Customers would call the team the moment they spotted a problem or saw a competitor doing something compelling. Customer churn has always been low as a result [1].

Despite small size, the team maintained a disciplined product release process while shipping code daily, sometimes multiple times per day [1].

### The Value of Low Investor Pressure

Not having heavy investor pressure early on allowed Snapdocs to experiment in an underappreciated part of the market. The Serviceable Available Market (SAM) of the initial product was roughly only $20M, but King believed it would allow expansion into more critical parts of the mortgage ecosystem [1]. He notes: "It was the type of opportunity that would be hard to discover through market analysis or spreadsheet exercises. You had to get deep into the problem set to see the opportunity" [1].

## Hiring Under Capital Constraints

Before the company could afford full-time employees, King worked with contractors and leaned on friends. Friends would come over to whiteboard or work through code problems [1].

When King could afford to hire, he treated early employees like founding team members — generous with equity and transparent about the business's potential and challenges. "Getting a few really good people into the company early on was foundational to the company's success" [1].

The first full-time hire was an engineer from a previous role (and one of the friends who helped in the early days). The second and third hires came from Hacker News job postings. All three turned out to be excellent [1].

### The Recruiting Regret

King's biggest early hiring mistake was being too timid to approach people he respected:

> "I should have tried to convince them to quit their successful jobs and join our small (yet risky at the time) startup. I'm fearless on this approach now, but back then I was intimidated to try to convince a friend to join a company that might fail. In hindsight I did them a disservice by not trying to recruit them." [1]

His lesson: smart people can evaluate the opportunity for themselves. Be honest about inherent challenges, but give them the opportunity to take a risk on you [1].

The team only hired when there was a clear and painful need. This made running the company harder but kept everyone working on the most important things [1].

## Navigating Market Cycles

A core lesson from Snapdocs is how to selectively respond to market changes. King describes the approach as "selectively ignoring some market changes, while reacting quickly to others" [1]. The filter is how a change aligns with resources, vision, and the north star metric of market share growth.

### Fluctuations in Mortgage Volume

The biggest dynamic change Snapdocs faces is fluctuations in monthly/annual mortgage volume, driven by economic factors. When well-resourced with momentum, the team ignores market downturns and stays focused on market share growth. Other times they scale up or back based on the fluctuation's size [1].

### Resisting Trendy Technology

Industry appetite for new technologies changes quickly. There were times when the market demanded a technology but King believed underlying industry factors would prevent it from scaling. Building it would pull resources from long-term priorities [1]:

> "Sometimes to the protests of our sales team, we ignored it or invested minimally in these trendy areas. By doing so, we were able to stay focused on the things that were truly going to transform the industry." [1]

### The Cost of Change Scales with Company Size

King notes that navigating change was relatively easy in the first few years when the team could all fit in one room, product cycles were short, and the cost of making a change was low. As the company grew, changes to speed or direction became more expensive: investments take longer to play out, headcount changes take longer to scale, and there are more layers to communicate through [1].

## The COVID Pivot (March 2020)

COVID accelerated demand for Snapdocs' product but shifted what customers wanted. The team had to expand quickly and pivot the roadmap immediately [1].

Decision-making worked in concentric circles [1]:
1. **Inner circle (3-4 people)**: Where the hardest and messiest conversations took place. Quickly defined problems, opportunities, and direction.
2. **Senior leadership team**: Further discussion and arming leaders with everything needed to communicate changes.
3. **Company-wide meeting**: Shared new direction and answered questions.

This entire process took about two weeks. For ongoing rapid changes, the company used temporary pods of 4-5 team members focused on specific challenges that would spin up for a few weeks and dissolve. Company-wide all-hands went from monthly to weekly [1].

### Intellectual Honesty About Growth

The team worked hard to be intellectually honest about COVID-driven growth:

> "This industry works in cycles. We're in an up cycle now and that's great. There's going to be a down cycle. We don't know when or how strong it's going to be. But we should not overly congratulate ourselves for the current situation, just as we shouldn't be too hard on ourselves when we're fighting through an inevitable downturn in the future." [1]

## Shifting Gears: From Efficient to Effective and Back

When Snapdocs raised its Series D in 2021, transactions had grown to millions of closings per year with thousands of lenders and title companies using the platform monthly. The company shifted from frugal to aggressive, raising larger rounds and hiring seasoned executives [1].

King describes the key question: "Is what I did yesterday the thing that's going to get me to where I need to be tomorrow?" The answer required changing parts of the culture and capital investment strategy [1].

But when mortgage demand cooled later that year, the company returned to its roots: ditched the motto of being effective over efficient, returned to ruthless prioritization, and shifted away from investing in future scale that would not be needed for years [1].

King's summary: "Market fluctuations are normal and unavoidable. Startups should scale up at times and scale back at others. It's hard and painful... But these ups and downs are natural and a necessary part of building an enduring company" [1].

## References

1. [Snapdocs' Aaron King on Navigating Market Cycles](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Gu-snapdocs-aaron-king-on-navigating-market-cycles) — [[Aaron King]], Lindsay Amos (2022)

title: Default Alive or Default Dead
aliases: do you make it to profitability
body:
# Default Alive or Default Dead

"Default Alive or Default Dead" is a diagnostic framework introduced by [[Paul Graham]] in October 2015 [1]. It poses a single question to startups past their first 8-9 months: assuming expenses remain constant and revenue growth continues on its current trend, does the company make it to profitability on the money it has left?

"Or to put it more dramatically, by default do they live or die?" [1]

## The Diagnostic

The question is binary. If default alive, you can discuss ambitious new things. If default dead, you need to talk about how to save the company [1].

Half the founders Graham talks to do not know the answer [1]. Trevor Blackwell built a [calculator](http://growth.tlb.org/#) to help founders determine their status.

The reason founders don't know: it is not a question that makes sense to ask early on, "any more than it makes sense to ask a 3-year-old how he plans to support himself" [1]. But as the company grows older, the question switches from meaningless to critical, and that switch often takes people by surprise [1].

Graham proposes starting to ask too early rather than too late. "It's probably not that dangerous to start worrying too early that you're default dead, whereas it's very dangerous to start worrying too late" [1].

## The Fatal Pinch

The most dangerous state is what Graham calls the "fatal pinch": default dead + slow growth + not enough time to fix it [1]. Founders end up there by not realizing that is where they are headed.

A secondary cause: founders assume it will be easy to raise more money. "But that assumption is often false, and worse still, the more you depend on it, the falser it becomes" [1].

Graham suggests making the assumption explicit: "Say 'We're default dead, but we're counting on investors to save us.' Maybe as you say that, it will set off the same alarms in your head that it does in mine" [1].

## The Investor Dependency Problem

It would be safe to be default dead if you could count on investors. But you cannot. Investor interest is a function of growth, and sometimes something about your business will spook investors even if growth is great [1].

The rule: fundraising should never be more than a plan A. "You should always have a plan B as well: you should know (as in write down) precisely what you'll need to do to survive if you can't raise more money, and precisely when you'll have to switch to plan B if plan A isn't working" [1].

Even with steep revenue growth (over 5x a year), you can only start to count on investor interest, never rely on it [1].

## Growth vs. Spending: The False Dichotomy

"In practice there is surprisingly little connection between how much a startup spends and how fast it grows" [1]. When growth is fast, it is because the product hits a nerve. When spending is high, it is because the product is expensive to build or sell, or the company is wasteful [1].

These are independent variables, not a tradeoff. The implication is that cutting spending rarely hurts growth and often does not affect it at all.

## Overhiring: The #1 Killer

"Don't hire too fast. Hiring too fast is by far the biggest killer of startups that raise money" [1].

Three reasons founders overhire:
1. There is so much work to do that they assume hiring will get it done
2. Successful startups have lots of employees, so it seems necessary
3. They do not want to face the real reason for slow growth: the product is not appealing enough [1]

VCs encourage overhiring because kill-or-cure strategies are optimal for portfolios. "VCs want to blow you up, in one sense of the phrase or the other. But as a founder your incentives are different. You want above all to survive" [1].

Sam Altman added: "I think it's roughly correct to say that YC's most successful companies have never been the fastest to hire, and one of the marks of a great founder is being able to resist this urge" [1].

## The Common Death Spiral

The typical pattern [1]:

1. Startup makes something moderately appealing
2. Decent initial growth
3. First round raised easily (founders seem smart, idea sounds plausible)
4. Growth is ok but not great
5. Founders convince themselves hiring will boost growth; investors agree
6. Growth still doesn't come (product is only moderately appealing)
7. Running out of runway with high expenses and slow growth
8. Unappealing to investors for next round
9. Company dies

What they should have done: address the fundamental problem with the product. Hire slowly. Seek growth by [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]], or by redesigning the product in the way only founders can [1].

## The Airbnb Counter-Example

Airbnb waited 4 months after raising money at the end of YC before hiring their first employee [1]. The founders were terribly overworked, but they were overworked evolving Airbnb into the organism it became. This is the model Graham holds up: founders doing the work themselves, staying lean, letting the product evolve before adding headcount [1].

## Premature Scaling

Paul Buchheit identifies a related pattern: "founders take a small business that isn't really working (bad unit economics, typically) and then scale it up because they want impressive growth numbers. This is similar to over-hiring in that it makes the business much harder to fix once it's big, plus they are bleeding cash really fast" [1].

## The Alternative Path

If you are default dead, instead of hiring to boost growth, Graham recommends [1]:
- Doing things that don't scale (see [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]])
- Redesigning the product -- something only founders can do effectively at the early stage
- Operating cheaply to extend runway
- Having an explicit written plan B for survival without more funding

The alarm bells from asking "am I default alive or dead?" can counteract the forces pushing you to overhire and instead compel you toward the paths to growth that actually work for most startups [1].

## References

1. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2015)

title: Equity Equation
body:
# Equity Equation

The Equity Equation is a formula introduced by [[Paul Graham]] in July 2007 for deciding whether any trade of company stock -- for investment, an employee, or a deal -- is worth making [1]. The formula is:

**1/(1 - n)**

where n is the fraction of the company you are giving up. You should make the trade if what you receive improves your average outcome enough that the (100 - n)% you have left is worth more than the whole company was before [1].

## How the Formula Works

If an investor wants to buy half your company, the investment must more than double your average outcome for you to break even. You have half as big a share of something that must be worth more than twice as much [1].

In the general case, if n is the fraction you are giving up, the deal is good if it makes the company worth more than 1/(1 - n) [1].

### Example: Y Combinator

At the time of writing, YC took about 7% of a company. n = 0.07, and 1/(1 - 0.07) = 1.075. So the deal is worth taking if YC can improve your average outcome by more than 7.5%. If they improve it by 10%, you are net ahead: the remaining 0.93 you hold is worth 0.93 x 1.1 = 1.023 [1].

Graham comments: "This is why we can't believe anyone would think Y Combinator was a bad deal. Does anyone really think we're so useless that in three months we can't improve a startup's prospects by 7.5%?" [1]

### Example: Sequoia

Greg McAdoo from Sequoia said at a YC dinner that when Sequoia invests alone they like to take about 30% of a company. 1/0.7 = 1.43, meaning the deal is worth taking if Sequoia can improve your outcome by more than 43% [1].

For the average startup, this would be an extraordinary bargain. "It would improve the average startup's prospects by more than 43% just to be able to say they were funded by Sequoia, even if they never actually got the money" [1]. The reason Sequoia is such a good deal is that their percentage is "artificially low" -- they limit holdings to leave founders enough stock to feel the company is still theirs [1].

The catch: Sequoia gets about 6,000 business plans a year and funds about 20. The odds of getting this deal are 1 in 300. The companies that make it through are not average startups [1].

## Applying the Formula to Employee Equity

The formula works in reverse for employees. If i is the average outcome multiplier with the addition of a new person, they are worth n such that i = 1/(1 - n), which means n = (i - 1)/i [1].

### Worked Example

Suppose you are two founders and want to hire a hacker so good you estimate he will increase the average outcome by 20%. n = (1.2 - 1)/1.2 = 0.167. You would break even trading 16.7% of the company for this person [1].

But 16.7% is not the right amount to offer because stock is not the only cost. There is salary and overhead too. Graham suggests multiplying the annual salary-plus-overhead rate by about 1.5 to translate it into stock terms. The reasoning: most startups grow fast or die. If they die, you don't pay. If they grow fast, next year's salary comes out of next year's (presumably 3x higher) valuation. So the total stock-equivalent cost is about 1.5 years at the present valuation [1].

Full example: the employee is worth 16.7% but the company wants 50% profit on the hire, so his "retail" price is about 11.1%. If he costs $60k/year in salary and overhead, times 1.5 = $90k total. At a $2M valuation, $90k is 4.5%. So the stock offer is 11.1% - 4.5% = 6.6% [1].

Graham notes: "notice how important it is for early employees to take little salary. It comes right out of stock that could otherwise be given to them" [1].

## The General Principle

"Whenever you make any decision involving equity, run it through 1/(1 - n) to see if it makes sense. You should always feel richer after trading equity. If the trade didn't increase the value of your remaining shares enough to put you net ahead, you wouldn't have (or shouldn't have) done it" [1].

The formula does not eliminate guesswork -- "ultimately you always have to guess" -- but it ensures you know what you are guessing about. Whether you use gut feel or a VC-supplied table of typical grant sizes, understand that those are estimates of the multiplier in the equity equation [1].

## Limitations

The formula assumes you can estimate the outcome multiplier, which is inherently uncertain. For VC deals, there are always factors beyond a straight trade of money for stock [1]. The present valuation (typically the post-money of your last round) probably undervalues the company because (a) the company has presumably grown since the last round and (b) the valuation of an early round usually reflects some other contribution by the investors [1].

## References

1. [The Equity Equation](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8u-the-equity-equation) -- [[Paul Graham]] (July 2007)

title: Feature Prioritization Frameworks
body:
# Feature Prioritization Frameworks

[[Emmett Shear]], co-founder of Justin.tv and Twitch, describes three mental frameworks for deciding what to build next. In his experience, startups progress through each framework in order as the type of challenge shifts [1].

Justin.tv started as a "Built For Me" product -- the founders wanted to make a live reality TV show and built the underlying technology for themselves. Twitch began the same way, as the only part of Justin.tv Shear personally enjoyed was video game streaming. When Twitch development started in earnest, the approach shifted to "Switch To Us" because the primary customers were streamers and Shear was not a streamer. Later, Twitch developed a "Three Numbers Matter" approach that drove most subsequent development [1].

## Built For Me

The founder is the primary exemplar user of the product. Deeply introspect and figure out what you wish the product did. Build that [1].

**When it works best:**
- When the founder truly loves the product. Shear managed the viewer side of the Twitch experience this way because he genuinely loved watching Twitch [1].
- With small teams. Intuitive, introspective understanding is hard to communicate to others. A small team inside a big company can use it, but a medium-sized organization cannot easily align on it [1].
- When the founder has good intuitive access to their desires. Some people want what they "should" want rather than what they actually want. Human introspection is unreliable -- "I think I want this because it's what I believe I'm supposed to want" feels subjectively identical to "I want this" [1].

**Why it gives startups an advantage:** Big companies cannot easily use this method because it does not scale. This explains why many of the biggest startups started with Built For Me -- it provides an automatic advantage against large incumbents. The ongoing popularity of "hack weeks" within large organizations is an attempt to unleash the Built For Me capacity latent in the organization by reverting to a more startup-like approach with small teams and intuitive decisions [1].

## Switch To Us

People are already doing the behavior the product enables, using competitors. The goal is to get them to do it with your product instead [1].

**The method:**
1. Interview 5-6 dedicated users each from your service, each competing service, and closely adjacent services
2. Accumulate approximately 50 interviews
3. Categorize responses and score them in a spreadsheet
4. Look for patterns. Consider doubling down on strengths and shoring up obvious weaknesses
5. Target one competitor at a time
6. Prioritize things that can be built or changed fast -- customers do not care how hard something is to build, only how effective it is for their problem [1]

**The critical principle for interviews:** Use interviews to understand the problem and generate ideas for solving it. "Validating an idea you already have is an anti-goal. You can't improve an idea through any amount of validation" [1]. The goal is to have better ideas as much as to prioritize them. If ideas are generated from customer problems and opportunities, both quality and intuitive prioritization improve [1].

**When it works best:**
- When the product is important to customers and they can articulate their needs. Twitch streamers had strong opinions about streaming services and could weight tradeoffs like "prosumers" or "SMBs." This would not work for something like Snapchat, where users may have no idea what they specifically like about it [1].
- When selling a better mousetrap, not inventing the category. Twitch was a better mousetrap for streamers on other platforms. It would not have worked for Justin.tv when there were no existing live streamers because they were the first live streaming service [1].

**Good interview questions:**
- What do you like about your current solution?
- What do not you like about it?
- How did you choose your current solution?
- What were you doing before?
- What would you do if you were the CEO of the company whose product you are using?
- What is the single most annoying thing about your current solution?
- What is the single coolest new thing about your current solution?
- Is there anything that would convince you to switch to us immediately if we built it?
- If you had a magic wand and could create any experience you wanted, how would it work?
- What is one thing about your current solution that is surprisingly hard?
- Follow up with "interesting, tell me more" or "why is that?" as appropriate. "People know more than they think they do, they just need a good interviewer to draw it out of them" [1]

## Three Numbers Matter

There are a few key drivers that make using the product better or worse. Identify the three most important and focus the entire organization on moving them [1].

**Examples:**
- **Amazon**: lower prices, more product selection, faster/more reliable shipping. Ideas that move those three numbers get priority [1].
- **Twitch**: audience size, positive interactions, money. Twitch focuses on helping streamers grow their audience, interact in more fun and connected ways, and earn more money per viewer [1].

**Why three:** "Three seems to be the maximum number of focus areas that people can keep in mind consistently." A list of "X, Y, and Z" feels natural; "W, X, Y, and Z" does not [1].

**The bonus fourth area: Stuff You Have To Do.** PCI compliance, uptime, security -- things that are not company focus areas but will kill the company if neglected. These should be done but not included in the top-level three metrics [1].

**The recursive property:** Each of the three top-level metrics can be further decomposed into three sub-drivers, and so on. Start with a metric for each area (these evolve -- revisit every few months), choose a resource split by intuition (maybe 33/33/33, maybe 50/25/25), and start trying to move the numbers [1].

**When it works best:**
- When people choose the product for measurable reasons (price, audience size, speed)
- Poorly suited for difficult-to-measure reasons (fun, connection, love, community). "Good for making utilitarian things, less good for making games and toys and beautiful UX" [1]

**Operational excellence:** Important for every company, but for a startup, winning through pure operational excellence is rare. Usually, the startup needs "good enough" operational excellence while focusing on what customers really care about. Sometimes one aspect of operational excellence (low latency, high security, super reliability) becomes a key goal, but even then most aspects should run on a "good enough" basis early on [1].

## Transitions Between Frameworks

Twitch periodically returned to Switch To Us for new customer segments even while running primarily on Three Numbers Matter. When launching a music category, the team talked to artists who livestreamed elsewhere to understand their needs. As the category grew, they began identifying key metrics and transitioning back to Three Numbers Matter [1].

## The Hard Problem

Certain products do not fit any framework: a brand-new category, where people like the product for hard-to-measure reasons, and the founder is not the end user. "It's really really hard!" [1]. This constrains big companies more than small ones because Built For Me does not scale. Big companies end up working on products with easily measurable drivers, or on incremental innovation. The exception is when the CEO is the lead designer (Steve Jobs at Apple) -- a big company escaping the dilemma to do ground-breaking category creation for products that are great for hard-to-measure reasons [1].

## References

1. [How to Prioritize Features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features) -- [[Emmett Shear]]

title: Three Traits of Great Leadership
body:
# Three Traits of Great Leadership

The Three Traits of Great Leadership is a measurement framework developed by [[Ali Rowghani]] based on his experience working closely with Ed Catmull, Steve Jobs, John Lasseter, and Bob Iger at Pixar and Disney [1]. Despite these four leaders being radically different in style and temperament, Rowghani found they all built extraordinary trust through the same three traits.

The framework's core claim: "You cannot be a great leader without [these three traits] because you cannot build trust without them" [1].

## The Three Traits

### 1. Clarity of Thought and Communication

Great leaders think clearly and describe a compelling vision [1]. If employees are "confused about your mission and strategy, or do not find it motivating or credible, they will not follow you with the focus and determination necessary to succeed" [1].

**How to improve**: Spend more time thinking about what is truly important, then practice expressing it simply [1]. Clarity of thought always precedes clarity of language.

**Exemplar**: Jeff Bezos's retail strategy at Amazon, built on three enduring customer preferences: lower prices, bigger selection, faster delivery. As Bezos said: "You can build a business strategy around the things that are stable in time" [1].

**At scale**: Great leaders spend hours preparing internal communications, regardless of natural talent. Tobi Lütke of Shopify spends hundreds of hours preparing for the annual employee Summit because "if we communicate well at the Summit, we achieve great alignment by the end" [1].

### 2. Judgment About People

Great leaders have strong intuition about who to empower. They "see hidden potential in people and detect cases where ambition exceeds ability" [1]. They also have the courage to correct hiring and promotion mistakes.

**The stakes**: "Nothing does more damage to an organization or to the standing of a leader than picking the wrong leaders or failing to correct these mistakes" [1]. But "leaders who fire too many of their own also lose a lot of credibility and trust" [1].

**How to improve**: Meet the best people in every field to sharpen pattern recognition. Do extensive reference checks. Learn from mistakes [1].

### 3. Personal Integrity and Commitment

Integrity means "standing for something meaningful beyond oneself" [1]. It includes admitting mistakes, receiving critical feedback openly, and avoiding trust-eroding behaviors (favoritism, conflicts of interest, inappropriate relationships).

**The test**: "if your team had full transparency into your private communications and behavior towards employees, would you be embarrassed by anything you have done or said?" [1].

Commitment goes beyond time investment. Great leaders "make their work into their core life mission in ways that inspire others" [1].

## Measuring Leadership with Surveys

Rowghani proposes structured employee surveys organized around the three traits. Startups should begin this at approximately 50 employees [1]. The survey design compares CEO self-assessment with employee perception to identify alignment gaps.

### Clarity of Thought Questions

**For the CEO** [1]:
- Write down your company's mission, strategy, and key metrics in less than 2 minutes
- Write down 2-3 themes you consistently emphasize in communications

**For employees** [1]:
- What is the company's mission and strategy?
- What are the most important operating metrics?
- How does your work contribute to key success metrics?
- How often has the definition of mission, strategy, and metrics changed in the past 24 months?
- What do you think is really important to the CEO?
- How effective is the CEO in written, large group, and small group communication?

### Judgment About People Questions

**For the CEO** [1]:
- Rate the effectiveness of each leader you have promoted or hired
- Name any leader you don't think is the right person for their role
- Have you exited the right employees?

**For employees** [1]:
- Has the CEO chosen good leaders?
- Which leaders do you respect and why?
- Are there leaders you think are weak and why?
- Were any recent leadership changes good decisions?
- Have high performers left the company in the past year? Why?
- (For departing employees) Are you leaving because of concerns about senior leadership?

### Personal Integrity Questions

**For the CEO** [1]:
- Have you taken actions that may have diminished employee confidence in your integrity?
- Do you ask for performance feedback? Can you cite examples of behavior change from feedback?
- How do you rate your commitment level? Your direct reports'?

**For employees** [1]:
- How would you rate the CEO's integrity/moral compass?
- Does the CEO listen well and respond to feedback?
- Have you seen favoritism, inappropriate relationships, conflicts of interest, or unethical behavior?
- What do employees feel motivates the CEO?
- How would you describe the CEO's personal commitment to the mission?
- Have you seen examples of lack of commitment from the CEO or other leaders?

## Why Not Measure by Business Results

Rowghani rejects measuring leadership through company performance because "timing and luck can be confounding factors" and because performance metrics provide "no clues as to how someone can improve as a leader" [1]. The three-trait framework is both diagnostic (where are you strong and weak?) and actionable (each trait has specific improvement paths).

## The Trust Equation

Trust is built through both competence (the "science") and character (the "art") [1]:

- **Science of trust**: Making accurate predictions about products, investments, and competitive landscapes. Choosing the right people for leadership roles. Getting things done.
- **Art of trust**: Saying the right thing at the right time. Standing for ideals bigger than yourself. Being honest about what you don't know. Not trying to be someone else.

"This is why you can't try to copy Steve Jobs or Ed Catmull in your quest to be a great leader. You can only be yourself" [1].

The operational lesson for difficult decisions: "ask yourself which course of action and which style of communication will increase the trust that employees have in them. When faced with a difficult challenge, they optimize for trust" [1].

## References

1. [How to Measure Leadership](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3j-how-to-measure-leadership) — [[Ali Rowghani]]

title: Mission-to-Metrics Framework
body:
# Mission-to-Metrics Framework

The Mission-to-Metrics framework is a three-level alignment tool described by [[Ali Rowghani]] as one of the three tasks that only a startup CEO can perform [1]. It connects the company's purpose (Mission) to its direction (Strategy) to its performance measurement (Metrics), creating a chain that allows every employee to understand how their work contributes to the company's goals.

## The Three Levels

[![Mission-to-Metrics Diagram](images/3k-the-second-job-of-a-startup-ceo/cb2bc3e4792d.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/11/Artboard-2white_wborder.png)

### Mission (Purpose)

The mission should feel "ambitious and permanent" and find its roots in the reasons the company was founded [1]. It should not change often.

### Strategy (Direction)

Product strategy and go-to-market strategy should be revisited at least twice per year to ensure relevance [1]. Rowghani strongly recommends writing strategy in long form rather than relying on slide decks: "the CEOs who are most effective in developing and communicating strategy take the time to write their strategy out, in long form" [1]. He cites Jeff Bezos's practice at Amazon of requiring 6-page memos for strategic meetings, though he notes founders need not go that far.

### Metrics (Pace and Performance)

A common mistake is equating key internal metrics with top-line results like revenue or user growth [1]. This fails because top-line results are usually not directly actionable. Instead, CEOs must dig deeper to find the drivers. Rowghani cites Facebook's discovery that connecting a new user to 10 friends within 14 days correlated with retained usage. Facebook then set "number of new users with 10 friend connections" as the key product metric [1].

"If you don't know what drives revenue, customer acquisition, or user growth, you aren't likely to be successful anyway" [1].

## The SpaceX Example

Rowghani's most vivid illustration comes from a friend who visited the SpaceX manufacturing floor [1]. He stopped to ask an employee assembling a large part, "What is your job at SpaceX?" The employee answered:

> "The mission of SpaceX is to colonize Mars. In order to colonize Mars, we need to build reusable rockets because it will otherwise be unaffordable for humans to travel to Mars and back. My job is to help design the steering system that enables our rockets to land back on earth. You'll know if I've succeeded if our rockets land on our platform in the Atlantic after launch."

The employee could have simply said he built a steering system. Instead, he traced his specific task through the strategy to the mission. "That is alignment" [1].

## Communication Is Constant

The framework only works if it is communicated relentlessly. Rowghani warns that founders must "reiterate the Mission-to-Metrics much more than what feels reasonable, which may run counter to your instinct to be efficient" [1].

The test is not whether employees can repeat the framework, but "whether they can make good decisions in your absence based on the context you have provided" [1].

This aligns with Aaron Epstein's advice on management communication: "You should feel like you sound like a broken record about the things that are important to your company" (see [[Startup Management]]).

## How to Build It

Rowghani's practical process [1]:

1. Write your Mission, Strategy, and Metrics in long form
2. Get feedback from your leadership team and key employees
3. Communicate it to everyone regularly
4. Test alignment by checking whether employees can make good decisions without you

## References

1. [The Second Job of a Startup CEO](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3k-the-second-job-of-a-startup-ceo) — [[Ali Rowghani]]

title: Legible Simple Obvious
body:
# Legible, Simple, Obvious

A framework from [[Kevin Hale]] for designing pitch deck slides that investors can understand and remember [1]. The framework is built on a single premise: before anyone can remember your ideas, they have to understand them. If a founder just communicates clearly, they will do better than 99% of startups.

## The Three Principles

**Legible**: The audience can physically read the slide. Use large type, bold text, a simple font, and good contrast from the background. Text at the top is easier to read from the back of a large room. At Demo Day, the room holds over 500 people, many of whom are older with imperfect eyesight [1].

**Simple**: Each slide expresses one idea. The word "simple" shares a root with "complex" -- the Latin "plex" meaning braid, twist, or fold. Simple ideas are "one fold." A simple slide does not intertwine multiple ideas. Since there are only five to seven key points, the deck should have only about five to seven slides [1].

**Obvious**: The slide can be understood at a glance. Test: show it to a stranger and ask what it means. If they do not immediately say your idea, you lose. Obviousness matters because Demo Day presentations are 2 minutes and 30 seconds, and investors are easily distracted. "If they don't get your point right away, they will check their email" [1].

## How to Apply

1. Start with legibility and simplicity.
2. Make the idea explicit. Add a direct caption or label that states the takeaway. Hale calls this "CliffsNotes right on the slide." A growth graph without labels requires study; a growth graph captioned "10x growth in 6 months" is instant [1].
3. Remove information distractions. Each additional piece of information increases comprehension time logarithmically ([Hick's Law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hick%27s_law)). Diagrams are "little mazes for ideas" [1].
4. Avoid screenshots, screencasts, and videos. They are almost always illegible, complex, and non-obvious. Replace with simplified bulleted steps describing the user flow [1].

## The One Exception

Deliberate complexity is acceptable when the point is to overwhelm the audience to illustrate how hard a problem is. Hale cites Magic (YC company), which showed a grid of every on-demand service logo across every industry to illustrate the scope of the problem their software solves [1].

## Anti-Patterns

- Too much text
- Excessive explanations and caveats
- Excessive branding per slide
- Photos without titles or captions
- Animations and transitions
- Memes, subtle humor, accidental humor

"Investors invest in teams not slides. Your slides should make your ideas more clear. Don't let your slides distract investors from what you're saying out loud" [1].

## References

1. [How to Design a Better Pitch Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck) — [[Kevin Hale]] (December 2015)

title: Schlep Filter
body:
# Schlep Filter

The Schlep Filter is a concept introduced by [[Paul Graham]] to describe an unconscious mental filter that prevents founders from seeing good startup ideas because those ideas involve tedious, unpleasant, or frightening work [1]. Graham wrote a [separate essay](http://www.paulgraham.com/schlep.html) on the condition it induces, which he calls "schlep blindness."

## The Mechanism

Most programmers wish they could start a startup by just writing brilliant code, pushing it to a server, and having users pay them lots of money [1]. They would prefer not to deal with tedious problems or get involved in messy ways with the real world. This is a reasonable preference, because such things slow you down. But this preference is so widespread that the space of convenient startup ideas has been stripped pretty clean [1].

"If you let your mind wander a few blocks down the street to the messy, tedious ideas, you'll find valuable ones just sitting there waiting to be implemented" [1].

## Stripe as the Canonical Example

Stripe is the most striking example of what happens when you turn off the schlep filter [1]. Thousands of programmers were in a position to see the payments opportunity. Thousands knew how painful it was to process payments before Stripe. But when they looked for startup ideas, they did not see this one, because unconsciously they shrank from having to deal with payments [1].

Dealing with payments is a schlep for Stripe, but not an intolerable one. The result of most people avoiding the idea: Stripe had comparatively smooth sailing in areas that are sometimes painful, like user acquisition. "They didn't have to try very hard to make themselves heard by users, because users were desperately waiting for what they were building" [1].

## The Unsexy Filter

The unsexy filter is a related but distinct concept [1]. While the schlep filter prevents you from working on problems you fear, the unsexy filter prevents you from working on problems you despise or find boring.

Graham and his co-founders overcame the unsexy filter to work on Viaweb. There were interesting things about the architecture of their software, but they were not interested in ecommerce per se. They could see the problem was one that needed to be solved [1].

Turning off the schlep filter is more important than turning off the unsexy filter for two reasons [1]:
1. The schlep filter is more likely to be an illusion -- the feared schleps may not be as bad as imagined
2. It is a worse form of self-indulgence -- "Starting a successful startup is going to be fairly laborious no matter what. Even if the product doesn't entail a lot of schleps, you'll still have plenty dealing with investors, hiring and firing people, and so on"

The unsexy filter, while still a source of error, is not entirely useless. If you are at the leading edge of a rapidly changing field, your ideas about what is sexy will be somewhat correlated with what is valuable. And if you find an idea sexy, you will work on it more enthusiastically [1].

## How to Overcome the Filters

Graham recommends several specific tactics [1]:

1. **Actively seek schlepy ideas.** Instead of merely turning off the filter, seek out ideas that are unsexy or involve schleps. "Don't try to start Twitter. Those ideas are so rare that you can't find them by looking for them. Make something unsexy that people will pay you for" [1].

2. **Ask what you wish someone else would build.** "What would you pay for right now?" This bypasses both the schlep and unsexy filters because you are imagining yourself as the customer [1].

3. **Look for dying industries.** "Since startups often garbage-collect broken companies and industries, it can be a good trick to look for those that are dying, or deserve to, and try to imagine what kind of company would profit from their demise" [1].

4. **Find disdained markets.** When big players ignore or look down on a market, that disdain often misleads them. HP turned down the Apple I because it used a TV as a monitor, which seemed intolerably declasse [1].

## The Connection to Starting a Startup Generally

Graham argues in his 2014 Stanford lecture that startups involve unglamorous but necessary schleps [2]. This is distinct from the bogus work that plagues big companies and school. "In a startup you have to spend a lot of time on schleps, but this sort of work is merely unglamorous, not bogus" [2].

The schlep filter is one of several reasons that the method of deliberately trying to think of startup ideas produces worse results than organic discovery. When you try to think of ideas, the schlep filter is active. When you naturally notice problems in your own life, you have already accepted the schlep because you are living with the problem [1].

## References

1. [How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas) -- [[Paul Graham]] (November 2012)
2. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2014)

title: Board Management
body:
# Board Management

Board management is one of the most anxiety-inducing responsibilities for startup founders. As [[Anu Hariharan]] describes it, the anxiety is natural: "this is the first time the founder/CEO is subject to external supervision, and the board has powers that include the firing of the CEO and the senior management" [1]. This article covers the full lifecycle of board management: composition, meeting cadence, deck preparation, leveraging board members, and handling difficult board dynamics.

## Purpose of a Board

The main role of a board is to guide the company through major decisions: hiring and firing senior management, approving corporate actions (compensation, stock options, budget), and offering guidance on strategic decisions that impact the business long-term [1]. Every Delaware corporation must have a board once it starts operating, but in the early days it consists of founders and functions mainly as a technical body for approvals like option grants [1].

When people refer to "setting up a board," they usually mean adding outside directors after raising money, either investor directors or independent directors [1].

## Board Composition

### Size and Evolution

- Early-stage companies: 3-5 board seats [1]
- Public companies: 7-9 seats [1]
- Series A and Series B lead investors typically join the board; later-stage growth investors generally do not ask for seats [1]
- Composition changes significantly in the years before going public, starting with recruiting independent board members, then forming committees (audit, compensation) [1]

### Selecting Board Members

The primary attribute to select for in early-stage board members is trust, and "the only way you build trust is with time" [1]. Hariharan recommends getting to know VC partners on your target list at least 6 to 9 months before your fundraise. Mathilde Colin, CEO of Front, documented her approach to this in detail [1].

Before adding new board members, founders should assess their strengths, weaknesses, and whether they would want to work with them for 10+ years, given that the median time to IPO can produce board relationships of that duration [1].

Reference checking is critical. However, many founders observe that this has caused some VCs to become less transparent in feedback with their portfolio companies, since they fear bad references affecting future deals. This dynamic has led some founders to bring on independent directors sooner [1].

### The Rise of Trusted Independents

Several examples illustrate the trend toward deeply trusted independent board directors [1]:
- **Atrium**: Founder Justin Kan added [[Michael Seibel]] (a former co-founder at Justin.tv) to the board at Series B
- **Brex**: Founders Henrique and Pedro added Victor Lazarte (TFG founder/CEO), who had known them since age 16
- **Faire**: Founders added Brian Grassadonia (Square Cash leader), their former manager at Square

These relationships are "likened to family relationships, where the family privately shares the kind of direct feedback no one else will" [1]. The caveat: "this does not mean you add your friend to your board. You need someone you really trust, who is 100% independent, and has the ability to help both you and your team as you scale the company" [1].

## Running Board Meetings

### Meeting Cadence

At the Series A stage, meetings tend to be informal (like 1:1s), ranging from bi-weekly to bi-monthly [1]. By Series B, establish a regular quarterly cadence with all meetings scheduled a year in advance to ensure attendance. In-person meetings are strongly preferred [1].

### Agenda and Length

Board meetings typically last about 3 hours [1]. The critical mistake to avoid is treating board meetings as status update meetings. Hariharan advises:

- First 45 minutes: highlights, lowlights, and KPIs [1]
- Remaining time: deep dives on no more than 1-2 strategic topics [1]

She shares the actual agenda from a Gusto board meeting as a model [1]:

| Agenda Item | Duration |
|---|---|
| Highlights / Lowlights | 10 min |
| Performance / KPIs | 50 min |
| Strategic topic 1: 3 Year Strategic Plan | 45 min |
| Strategic topic 2: Engineering Org | 45 min |
| Closed Session | 30 min |

The result: no more than one-third of the meeting on the state of the business, with the majority on strategic issues [1].

Hariharan recommends identifying and aligning on key KPIs with board members early, as this "trains" board members to process operational data quickly and focus on the "why" behind hits and misses [1].

### Pre-Meeting Preparation

What makes Gusto's approach work happens outside the board meeting [1]:
1. Extensive prep on areas where input is sought (ideally topics already being discussed with the executive team, so materials serve double duty)
2. Pre-read deck sent at least one week in advance
3. Questions collected from each board member in a Google Doc three days before
4. Questions answered in the Google Doc by the morning of the meeting

Brex follows an even more structured 28-day preparation timeline [1]:

| Timing | Activity |
|---|---|
| T-28 days | Founders/Chief of Staff identify strategic topics; align with executive team |
| T-18 days | Outline topics, write content, share draft deck with executive team |
| T-11 days | Execs provide feedback; Finance finalizes deck |
| T-7 days | Final review; distribute deck to board |
| T-4 days | Board submits questions (especially on KPIs) |
| T-2 days | Brex consolidates and answers all board questions |
| T-0 | Set expectations upfront on where to spend time and seek feedback |

### Board Deck Structure

The board deck is "just a tool for discussion, not an end in and of itself" [1]. Its structure should align with the meeting agenda. Gusto's decks are 30-50 pages and follow this structure [1]:

- **Highlights / Lowlights**: 1 page
- **Performance / KPIs**: 15-20 pages
- **Strategic Topic 1**: 5-10 pages covering options considered, decision framework, product roadmap (including what they plan NOT to pursue), known vs. unknown risks, economic value assessment, discussion questions
- **Strategic Topic 2**: 5-10 pages covering current org, hiring scale, future org (18 months), talent assessment, retention/development/recruitment plan
- **Appendix**: 10 slides on organizational health (hiring metrics, close rates, employee pulse survey results)

### Involving Executives

Post-Series B, Hariharan recommends having specific executives join only for their agenda item (about 45 minutes) rather than attending the entire meeting [1]. The first approach (all executives attending) tends to turn the meeting into a presentation rather than a discussion and makes it harder for the CEO to discuss sensitive matters [1].

The targeted approach achieves three things: deeper discussion on strategic issues, executives hearing board feedback directly, and more time for the CEO and board to discuss sensitive matters privately [1].

General counsel and/or outside counsel should attend all board meetings to capture notes and handle issues requiring attorney-client privilege [1].

## Monthly Updates

In addition to quarterly meetings, a 2-page monthly update email at the beginning of every month is best practice [1]. The email typically includes: last month's performance summary, hiring update, burning issues, and specific asks for help.

Practical examples of effective asks [1]:
- **Brex**: Always makes two specific asks in monthly emails: introductions to business partners/customers, and help with key open roles
- **GitLab**: Asks for introductions to specific individuals at potential customer companies, and publicly applauds investors who have helped, "putting pressure on the entire board to do the same"

Monthly updates accomplish three things [1]:
1. Board members go above and beyond to help with specific asks
2. The board arrives better prepared for quarterly meetings
3. The CEO is forced to step back and objectively assess the business

Hariharan cites Jeff Bezos's advocacy for written narrative over slides: "written narrative is more compelling in sharing ideas than bullet points and slides" [1].

### Letter to the Board

Some CEOs use the board meeting as an occasion for deeper reflection [1]:
- **Peter Reinhardt** (Segment): Writes a 3-5 page memo covering highlights, lowlights, and time allocation analysis, including his own strengths and weaknesses as CEO
- **Faire**: Uses written memos to discuss strategic priorities, articulating decision frameworks and documenting uncertainties and blind spots. These memos help the board prepare and create a record of decision-making quality

## Navigating Board Dynamics

### Mixed Messages

Conflicting viewpoints are productive: "The job of the board is to push you on your strategy and to ask questions that help sharpen your thinking" [1]. CEOs should welcome disagreement and not feel pressure to reach consensus.

When conflict stalls progress, take the conversation offline with 1:1 or small group meetings. Use a decision-making framework (e.g., economic value, resources, cash needs, competitive threat) to guide the path forward [1].

A critical principle: "Your board members will never be as close to the business to make that decision. While board members provide important input all CEOs should seriously consider, the CEO has the authority to make the best decision on behalf of the company" [1].

### Asking for Help

Not every board member needs to be in the loop on every topic. "It is a poor use of time to update every board member 1:1 before every board meeting" [1]. Instead, follow up directly with specific board members on topics where they can be most helpful.

### Balancing Helpfulness vs. Control

"Be cautious of board members who are trying to make decisions for you. A board member's job is to help you think through the issues by offering tools or decision frameworks -- not to give you all the answers on how to run your business" [1].

Hariharan cites Micky Malka of Ribbit Capital as an exemplary board member: when the Brex CEO asked about typical credit loss rates, rather than giving a definitive answer, Malka shared examples from 10 startups in Ribbit's portfolio with their trajectories and pros/cons of each approach, empowering the founders to decide [1].

### Problem Board Members

If a board member is unhelpful or overbearing [1]:
1. First attempt a 1:1 meeting with constructive, specific feedback
2. Work with other board members to understand and address the issue
3. If behavior is destructive (meddling, micromanaging, leaking to press), consider either approaching someone senior at the board member's firm for a replacement, or adding more board members to create counterweight

The warning: if all board members echo the same feedback and the CEO is not listening, "the problem is likely you" [1]. Some CEOs bring executive coaches to board meetings to calibrate their perception of board dynamics.

### Removing Board Members

This is a last resort with two options [1]:
1. **Approach the firm for a replacement**: Burns bridges but works if the company is performing well (high leverage)
2. **Add more independent directors**: Creates counterweight without direct confrontation

Companies that are top performers in a VC's portfolio have more leverage than they realize in requesting board member changes [1].

## References

1. [How to Create and Manage a Board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board) — [[Anu Hariharan]]

title: University Spinouts
body:
# University Spinouts

[[Jared Friedman]], YC partner, provides advice for people who have done scientific research at a university and are considering starting a company to commercialize it. YC has funded more than 75 companies in this situation. YC also conducted a [Bio Tour](https://blog.ycombinator.com/yc-bio-tour-2020/) visiting research universities to talk with hundreds of students and professors in the life sciences about commercialization [1].

## Deciding Who Should Spin Out

In a typical spinout, several people worked on the research -- a mix of students, post-docs, and faculty. The first decision is who works on the company and who stays at the university. Friedman identifies four common misconceptions [1]:

### Misconception 1: You Can Start a Company While Continuing Your Academic Career

"Your company has little chance of success unless someone who worked on the original research is willing to leave their university role to start this company" [1]. Students graduating soon can wait until graduation -- a perfect time to start. Otherwise, an intentional decision to leave must be made at some point [1].

### Misconception 2: You Should Find a CEO to Run the Company

Scientists who remain in academia and try to find a CEO to build a company around their invention almost always fail. Great CEOs are scarce and have better opportunities than running an idea-stage startup with no funding. Most scientists either never find a CEO or settle for a mediocre one. It is even worse if the university tries to find a CEO [1].

Even with any person in the world available, an outside CEO would likely be a bad choice. The people who did the original research are far more invested and far more qualified -- their domain knowledge is more valuable than general business skills. The common belief that "the research is done and all that's left is commercialization" rarely holds. Usually the market wants something different from what was invented, requiring more research. Original inventors can adjust; an outside CEO will be stuck [1].

### Misconception 3: You Need Someone With Business Experience on the Founding Team

"In the first couple of years, there is typically very little 'business' to be done, and whatever business skills you need you will pick up along the way" [1]. Most scientists YC funds have no prior business experience. "People who work in business like to make it sound hard, as if business were like quantum physics, a field that needed to be studied for years to master. The fact is, it's not even close" [1].

### Misconception 4: You Should Raise Money First, Then Leave the University

Scientists often pitch to local VCs hoping for validation and a smooth transition. VCs occasionally fund spinouts this way but usually do not. Founders who get rejected conclude the idea is bad and give up, when the actual issue is that it is too early for VC money [1].

Typically founders need to work full-time for 1+ years before a multi-million dollar VC round is feasible. In the meantime, they sustain themselves through savings, government grants, friends and family, or small pre-seed rounds from angels, accelerators, or seed funds [1].

Founders who refuse to quit before raising face a catch-22: "They are waiting for an investor to take a bet on them before they quit their job. But the investors are waiting for the founders to believe enough in their own company to quit their job" [1].

### The Recommended Approach

The ideal situation: two or more people from the lab who did the work leave together as cofounders. One full-time founder is also acceptable. One of the people who leave should be CEO. Others who remain at the university can serve as "academic cofounders" or "scientific cofounders" and contribute, but the full-time founders are the most important [1].

## Deciding When to Spin Out

In the early stages of developing a new technology, progress is faster at the university, taking advantage of university resources. At some point that flips, and being at the university slows things down because universities are not set up to commercialize technologies [1].

Leaving too early is possible, but the far more common mistake is waiting too long. Most founders delay because leaving is scary. "No one is pressuring you to leave and leaving seems risky so the natural thing to do is to keep delaying it" [1]. The temptation is to make the technology perfect before spinning out, with always "one more experiment" to do. Programs like NSF I-Corps can test market demand from inside the university [1].

After leaving, founders often realize that much of the last year's work was wasted because assumptions about market needs were wrong, and that they are now moving so much faster that they could have saved months by spinning out a year earlier [1].

## Splitting Equity

Friedman's framework has two rules [1]:

1. **Full-time founders get equal or nearly equal equity.** (He references [[Michael Seibel]]'s [essay](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5x-how-to-split-equity-among-co-founders) on this.)
2. **Full-time founders get much more than academic cofounders.** Academic cofounders should typically own no more than 10% unless they remain hands-on.

The biggest conceptual mistake: thinking equity allocation rewards past contributions, when it actually anticipates future ones. "If you are going to make a new company successful, you will probably have to work on it for 7-10 years post spin-out" [1].

Friedman uses a marathon analogy: at spinout, founders may feel halfway done, but they are actually on mile 2 of 26. Academic founders may have been instrumental in mile 1, but full-time founders take the other 25. The equity split must reflect expected contributions over the entire marathon [1].

This means equity in the new company may have no relation to academic seniority. Junior people who leave often get much more equity than their former senior faculty boss. "This can be an awkward conversation, but it's entirely sensible" [1].

## Negotiating with Tech Transfer Offices

Commercializing university research requires negotiating intellectual property rights with the technology transfer office (TTO) [1].

TTOs historically had a bad reputation for being slow, bureaucratic, and forcing onerous terms that strangled young startups. Things have improved. TTOs at universities in major startup hubs (Harvard, MIT, Stanford) now give startups reasonable terms, though they still take too long. At universities with fewer successful spinouts, quality is hit-or-miss. Some universities now use "[express license agreements](https://ctl.cornell.edu/entrepreneurs/fasttrack/)" -- preset agreements requiring little to no negotiation [1].

### Four Key Terms

1. **Equity**: University gets equity in the company. 3-5% is typical and acceptable. Above 10% causes problems [1].
2. **Royalty**: Percentage of revenue or profits paid to the university. If too high, it affects the company's ability to raise money and operate. Ideally zero; keep it as low as possible [1].
3. **Milestone payments** and **sublicensing terms** (details truncated in source) [1].

## References

1. [How to Spin Your Scientific Research Out of a University and Into a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8f-how-to-spin-your-scientific-research-out-of-a-university-and-into-a-startup) -- [[Jared Friedman]]

title: Founder Mindset
body:
# Founder Mindset

[[Paul Graham]] returns repeatedly across a decade of essays to the question of what makes a good startup founder. His answer is consistent: determination matters more than intelligence, and the best two-word description of the ideal founder is "relentlessly resourceful" [1].

## Relentlessly Resourceful

In a March 2009 essay, Graham distilled the quality he looks for in founders into two words [1]. The opposite of a good founder is "hapless" -- passive, battered by circumstances, letting the world have its way with you instead of having your way with the world [1].

A good running back is not merely determined but flexible. They want to get downfield, but they adapt their plans on the fly [1]. Similarly, a good founder is not merely relentless but resourceful. In any interesting domain, the difficulties are novel. "You don't know initially how hard they are; you don't know whether you're about to plow through a block of foam or granite. So you have to be resourceful. You have to keep trying new things" [1].

Graham makes the relationship between his two most famous pieces of advice explicit: "'Make something people want' is the destination, but 'Be relentlessly resourceful' is how you get there" [1].

This quality is specific to startups. Being relentlessly resourceful is not the recipe for success in writing or painting, where the obstacles are mostly internal (your own obtuseness) rather than external [1]. And it is "definitely not the recipe for success in big companies" [1].

Can it be taught? After four years of trying at YC, Graham concluded: "yes, surprisingly often it can. Not to everyone, but to many people" [1]. Some people are constitutionally passive, but others have a latent ability that only needs to be brought out, particularly young people who have always been under the thumb of authority [1].

## Determination Over Intelligence

Graham's 2006 essay on the hardest startup lessons states it plainly: "The most important quality in a startup founder is determination. Not intelligence -- determination" [2].

He found this somewhat depressing. "I'd like to believe Viaweb succeeded because we were smart, not merely determined" [2]. VCs make the same mistake, investing in startups founded by eminent professors when in software they should be investing in students. Microsoft, Yahoo, and Google were all founded by people who dropped out of school. "What students lack in experience they more than make up in dedication" [2].

The relationship between intelligence and determination: "You can lose quite a lot in the brains department and it won't kill you. But lose even a little bit in the commitment department, and that will kill you very rapidly" [2].

Graham illustrates the demands with a vivid picture of startup life: "Imagine if you were hired at some big company, and in addition to writing software ten times faster than you'd ever had to before, they expected you to answer support calls, administer the servers, design the web site, cold-call customers, find the company office space, and go out and get everyone lunch" [2]. And this against a backdrop of constant disasters.

## Commitment as Self-Fulfilling Prophecy

Commitment creates its own momentum [2]. If you make it clear you are going to succeed no matter what, acquirers are more likely to buy you (because if they don't and you stick around, your price will go up), and investors are more likely to fund you (because "what really motivates investors, even big VCs, is not the hope of good returns, but the fear of missing out") [2].

"You can't fake this. The only way to convince everyone that you're ready to fight to the death is actually to be ready to" [2].

But determination must be the right kind. Graham carefully chose "determined" rather than "stubborn" because stubbornness is disastrous in a startup [2]. The running back metaphor again: a successful running back improvises. If someone appears in front of him, he runs around them. If someone tries to grab him, he spins out of their grip. He will even run in the wrong direction briefly if that helps. "The one thing he'll never do is stand still" [2].

## Startups Are Counterintuitive

Graham's 2014 Stanford lecture (for Sam Altman's startup class) opens with the claim that startups are as counterintuitive as skiing [4]. When you first try to slow down on skis, your instinct is to lean back, which makes you fly down the hill out of control. Startups have a similar list of instincts you must override [4].

Six counterintuitive points [4]:

1. **Your instincts will mislead you** about startups specifically, though you can trust them about people. If someone seems slippery or bogus, trust your gut. "Work with people you genuinely like, and you've known long enough to be sure" [4].

2. **Expertise in startups is not what matters.** The way to succeed is not to be an expert on startups but to be an expert on your users. Mark Zuckerberg succeeded despite being "a complete noob at startups" because he understood his users well [4]. If you meet an undergrad who knows all about convertible notes and class FF stock, that sets off alarms -- they are probably "playing house" [4].

3. **Gaming the system stops working.** There is no boss to trick, only users. Startups are as impersonal as physics. "You have to make something people want, and you prosper only to the extent you do" [4].

4. **Startups are all-consuming.** If your startup succeeds, it will take over your life for several years at the very least, maybe for a decade. Larry Page started running as fast as he could at 25 and it must seem like he hasn't stopped. "Every day new shit happens in the Google empire that only the CEO can deal with" [4].

5. **You can't tell if you're up to it.** For the past 9 years, Graham's job was to predict whether people would succeed as founders. "The hard part was predicting how tough and ambitious they would become. There may be no one who has more experience at trying to predict that, so I can tell you how much an expert can know about it, and the answer is: not much" [4].
