6. **The way to get startup ideas is not to try to think of them.** See [[Startup Ideas]] for the full treatment.

## The Half-Hearted Effort Problem

The most common type of startup failure is not the spectacular flameout but the one that never really gets going -- "some project a couple guys started on the side while working on their day jobs, but which never got anywhere and was gradually abandoned" [5].

Graham argues that statistically, if you want to avoid failure, the most important thing is to quit your day job. Most founders of failed startups don't quit their day jobs; most founders of successful ones do [5]. "If startup failure were a disease, the CDC would be issuing bulletins warning people to avoid day jobs" [5].

"Most startups fail because they don't make something people want, and the reason most don't is that they don't try hard enough. In other words, starting startups is just like everything else. The biggest mistake you can make is not to try hard enough" [5].

## Fear the Right Things

Founders are right to be paranoid, but they sometimes fear the wrong things [2]. Most visible disasters -- a founder quits, you discover a patent, your servers crash, you have to change your name, a deal falls through -- are par for the course. They will not kill you unless you let them [2].

Big companies are not what you should fear. "The people at Google are smart, but no smarter than you; they're not as motivated, because Google is not going to go out of business if this one product fails; and even at Google they have a lot of bureaucracy to slow them down" [2].

The real danger: other startups you don't know exist yet, and more importantly, self-inflicted wounds. Internal disputes, inertia, and ignoring users -- each is by itself enough to kill you [2]. The worst: "a couple of founders who have some great idea they know everyone is going to love, and that's what they're going to build, no matter what" [2].

## Don't Get Your Hopes Up

This was practically the corporate motto at Viaweb [2]. Startup founders are naturally optimistic -- they wouldn't do it otherwise. "But you should treat your optimism the way you'd treat the core of a nuclear reactor: as a source of power that's also very dangerous. You have to build a shield around it, or it will fry you" [2].

The shield should be pierced selectively: be optimistic about what you can do, but assume the worst about machines and other people [2].

This is particularly important with deals. "If your startup is doing a deal, just assume it's not going to happen. The VCs who say they're going to invest in you aren't. The company that says they're going to buy you isn't. The big customer who wants to use your system in their whole company won't" [2].

The practical reason is not to prevent disappointment but to prevent dependence. If someone says they want to invest, the natural tendency is to stop looking for other investors. The best leverage in any deal is genuinely not needing it [2].

"The way to succeed in a startup is to focus on the goal of getting lots of users, and keep walking swiftly toward it while investors and acquirers scurry alongside trying to wave money in your face" [2].

## Speed, Not Money

Graham reframes the purpose of startups: economically, a startup is best seen not as a way to get rich but as a way to work faster [2]. You have to make a living, and a startup is a way to get that done quickly, instead of letting it drag on through your whole life [2].

"By compressing the dull but necessary task of making a living into the smallest possible time, you show respect for life, and there is something grand about that" [2].

## References

1. [Be Relentlessly Resourceful](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/94-be-relentlessly-resourceful) -- [[Paul Graham]] (March 2009)
2. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2006)
3. [Why to Not Not Start a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (March 2007)
4. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2014)
5. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
6. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)

title: Biotech Startup Funding
body:
# Biotech Startup Funding

[[Jared Friedman]], YC partner, argues that biotech startup fundraising in 2019 looks like tech startup fundraising looked in 2005, and the same forces that transformed tech fundraising are now transforming biotech [1]. The parallel is structural: new infrastructure has collapsed the cost to start, which enables new funding models, which shifts power to founders.

## How Tech Fundraising Changed (2005-2019)

In 2005, when YC started, venture capital access was limited. VCs preferred companies that already seemed like a sure bet, favored MBAs with executive experience over unproven technical founders, asked for onerous financial terms, and often replaced founders with favored executives. The only model of institutional seed funding was the "business incubator" where VC firms funded well-connected founders and incubated them in their offices [1].

Then the cost to start a tech company plummeted. Open source software, modern web frameworks, SaaS developer tools, cloud hosting, and better distribution channels meant technical founders could launch a product and get users with minimal funding. Once they proved the idea had merit, they could use traction to raise capital [1].

These companies needed only a small amount of money to get started, but no institutional investors made small investments. This insight led to the creation of YC and hundreds of institutional seed funds. Easy access to flexible institutional seed funding led to an explosion of tech startups [1].

Because founders raised VC only when they were further along and had leverage, the balance of power shifted. Founders increasingly retained control. Investors lost the power to fire founders and bring in executives. And when founders did run their companies, investors discovered something surprising: "despite their inexperience, the founders were often the right people to run the company" [1]. (Friedman notes this trend was accelerated by institutional seed funding, building on the logic Ben Horowitz [articulated in 2010](https://a16z.com/2010/04/28/why-we-prefer-founding-ceos/) about preferring founding CEOs.)

## The Biotech Parallel

Early-stage biotech funding in 2019 is dominated by the "venture creation model." The VC firm creates the company, assembles a team of favored executives (often from their entrepreneur-in-residence pool), incubates the startup out of the VC's offices, invests a large amount of money upfront, and takes a controlling ownership stake [1].

This model made sense when the cost to start a biotech company was high -- when no one could make progress before a VC wrote a $10M check. But new infrastructure has brought down the cost of doing biology dramatically. Founders can now make real progress proving a concept for as little as $100K [1].

### Infrastructure That Lowered Costs

Friedman catalogs the new infrastructure [1]:

- **Low-cost CROs** (contract research organizations): [WuXi Biologics](http://wuxibiologics.com), [Evotec](http://evotec.com), [ChemPartner](http://chempartner.com)
- **CRO access platforms**: [Science Exchange](https://www.scienceexchange.com/) for instantaneous, cost-effective access to CROs and scientific supplies
- **Lab space by the bench**: [MBC BioLabs](https://mbcbiolabs.com/), [Berkeley BioLabs](http://www.berkeleybiolabs.com/)
- **Lab management**: [Quartzy](https://www.quartzy.com/), [Happilabs](https://www.happilabs.org/)
- **Lab automation**: Affordable lab robots from [OpenTrons](https://opentrons.com/) for automating batch experiments
- **Computational drug discovery**: [Atomwise](https://www.atomwise.com/) for in silico experiments
- **Patent filing**: [Cognition IP](https://www.cognitionip.com/) for lower-cost patents
- **FDA submission**: [Enzyme](https://www.enzyme.com/) for streamlined regulatory submission

### YC Bio Companies as Evidence

Bio companies routinely clear major scientific hurdles during YC's short program. Therapeutics companies show effectiveness in animal models. Diagnostic companies show success with human samples. Synthetic biology companies successfully engineer cell lines [1].

**Shasqi** (2015): Jose Mejia Oneto, an MD/PhD who left orthopaedic surgery residency, had developed a technique for localizing chemotherapy delivery in academia but had not tried it in animal therapeutics. Using just YC's funding, he showed in less than three months in a breast cancer mouse model that localized delivery outperformed conventional chemo [1].

**Athelas**: Founders Tanay Tandon and Deepika started the company while still in college, making a device for at-home blood tests for oncology patients using computer vision. They built a working prototype with $40K in investment. During YC they ran a 350-patient initial study showing very good results. The device is now FDA-cleared and serves thousands of patients [1].

Friedman notes that running clinical trials for drugs remains very expensive, and biotech companies ultimately need to raise large amounts. But this is not different from top tech companies: the biggest YC software companies each raised over $1B. The key is that these companies could get started with less than $100K and de-risk enough to raise more [1].

## Predictions

Because startups can start cheaply, the tech startup model is now available for biotech: raise money incrementally, keep control, and work on your own idea rather than VC-originated ideas [1].

This path has drawn a new kind of biotech founder. Many are grad students or postdocs whose previous options were staying in academia or joining big pharma. Starting a company is now a viable third option [1].

If the pattern plays out as it did in tech [1]:
- An explosion of funding options for biotech companies
- Traditional biotech investors' controlling legal terms will go out of vogue, as happened in tech in the early 2000s
- A new crop of biotech and tech/biotech crossover seed funds will create a vibrant seed ecosystem (YC bio companies already typically raise $1-5M seed rounds after each batch)
- More biotech companies will be run by founders who care about their ideas, not by VCs and hired executives

## References

1. [How Biotech Startup Funding Will Change in the Next 10 Years](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4L-how-biotech-startup-funding-will-change-in-the-next-10-years) — [[Jared Friedman]] (February 2019)

title: China Tech Ecosystem
body:
# China Tech Ecosystem

The Chinese technology ecosystem has produced several of the world's most innovative consumer technology companies, many of which pioneered product strategies years before Western equivalents adopted them. [[Anu Hariharan]] has documented three Chinese tech case studies in detail for the YC Library: [[WeChat]] (messaging as a platform), [[Pinduoduo]] (social commerce), and [[Toutiao]] (AI-powered content). Together, these case studies reveal patterns that are instructive for founders globally.

## Key Structural Differences from the US Market

### Mobile Leapfrogging

China's mobile revolution was more dramatic than the West's. Smartphone penetration went from near zero in 2010 to 65% by 2014 [3]. Many consumers skipped the desktop internet entirely, creating massive demand for mobile-first services. Toutiao exploited this gap: by mid-2012, there were only six significant news apps on the Chinese Android platform [3].

### Platform Openness

[[WeChat]] functions as the operating system of Chinese mobile life, and Tencent has been willing to let third-party services grow on top of it. Pinduoduo's rapid growth was significantly enabled by access to WeChat's social graph and messaging infrastructure [1]. This contrasts with the US, where Hariharan notes that "it is highly unlikely that Facebook would allow a social commerce application like Pinduoduo to be built on top of Messenger or Instagram" [1].

### QR Code Adoption

QR codes became the bridge between offline and online commerce in China through WeChat, but failed in the US for three reasons: the dominant phone and social app did not support scanning, users needed dedicated scanner apps, and early use cases focused on spam-like marketing content [2].

### Unorganized Retail

More than 80% of retail in China's developing cities is unorganized [1]. This creates a "trust deficit" that social commerce platforms address by embedding purchases within social relationships. WeChat Red Packets similarly built payment adoption through trusted social circles first, then extended to merchants [2].

## Recurring Growth Patterns

Three patterns appear across all three case studies:

### 1. Self-Cannibalization

Tencent launched WeChat as a potential competitor to its own QQ, with a small skunkworks team of seven engineers from the QQ Mail division [2]. This echoes Amazon's self-cannibalization with Kindle and Uber's move from town cars to UberX. The lesson: "If you don't create a culture of disrupting yourself, then a competitor is likely to disrupt you" [2].

### 2. Group-Level Network Effects

Both [[WeChat]] and [[Pinduoduo]] designed features around group behavior rather than individual behavior [1] [2]. WeChat's Allen Zhang was "adamant about developing the product around 'Group Effect'" [2], and Pinduoduo's team purchase model follows Reed's law (value proportional to group formation ease) [1]. Toutiao achieved similar engagement levels without a social graph, but its content lifecycle creates an analogous data network effect [3].

### 3. Subtle Monetization

All three platforms aligned monetization with user experience rather than extracting value from it. WeChat limited Moments ads to one per day [2]. Toutiao's ad targeting uses the same recommendation engine as content, making ads feel like product discovery [3]. Pinduoduo's gamification features tie engagement to commerce without requiring immediate purchases [1].

## Implications for Western Founders

Hariharan consistently argues that Chinese tech innovations preview global trends. The success of social commerce "will be replicated by others globally" [1]. Content recommendation engines like Toutiao's suggest that "content aggregators" may be "an idea whose time is yet to come -- and that better algorithms will be the catalyst for success" [3].

The specific mechanics (team purchase, Red Packets, QR codes) may be culturally specific, but the underlying insights about social dynamics in commerce, group-level network effects, and AI-driven personalization are "likely entirely universal" [1].

## References

1. [Pinduoduo and the Rise of Social E-Commerce](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce) — [[Anu Hariharan]], Nic Dardenne
2. [How WeChat Grew to Be the #1 App in the World](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world) — [[Anu Hariharan]]
3. [The Hidden Forces Behind China's Content King Toutiao](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao) — [[Anu Hariharan]]

title: Equity Split
body:
# Equity Split

Equity split refers to how ownership of a startup is divided among co-founders and early contributors. It is one of the most consequential early decisions a startup makes and one of the most common sources of co-founder conflict.

## The Default: Equal Splits

Y Combinator's general guidance favors equal or near-equal splits among co-founders. The 10 questions to discuss with a potential co-founder include equity as a core topic, and the implication is that significant imbalances create resentment and misalignment [1].

[[Jared Friedman]] notes that equity split is particularly fraught in [[University Spinouts]], where the question of how much equity the university, the professor, and the founding team each receive can become contentious. Universities typically demand significant equity (often 5-15% or more) plus licensing fees, which can create conflicts with venture investors who expect clean cap tables [2].

## The Co-Founder Conversation

The [[Co-Founders]] article covers the broader question of co-founder selection. Equity split should be one of the earliest conversations, addressed before the company is incorporated. Key considerations include:

- Relative contributions (past work on the idea vs. future contributions)
- Full-time vs. part-time commitment
- Vesting schedules (standard is 4-year vesting with 1-year cliff)
- What happens if a co-founder leaves

## The Equity Equation for Non-Founders

[[Paul Graham]]'s [[Equity Equation]] provides a mathematical framework for evaluating any equity trade: if someone joining the company makes your stock worth more than 1/(1-n) where n is the fraction you are giving up, the trade is worth it. This applies to early employees, advisors, and anyone receiving equity.

## References

1. [10 Questions to Discuss with a Potential Co-Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Hj-10-questions-to-discuss-with-a-potential-co-founder) -- Y Combinator (2022)
2. [How to Spin Your Scientific Research Out of a University and Into a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8f-how-to-spin-your-scientific-research-out-of-a-university-and-into-a-startup) -- [[Jared Friedman]]

title: Scaling and Later-Stage Operations
body:
# Scaling and Later-Stage Operations

[[Sam Altman]] outlines common issues founders face when their startups are 12-24 months in and doing well. The central shift: before [[Product-Market Fit]], the number one job is building a great product. As the company grows past approximately 25 employees, the main job shifts from building a great product to building a great company [1].

## Management

### The Transition Point

There is very little management in the beginning, and that works well. When there are fewer than 20 employees, most companies have everyone report to the founder. That is optimal for the early stage. But what works at 20 employees can be disastrous at 40. Structure must be in place before it is painfully necessary [1].

The starting structure is simple: every employee knows who their manager is, everyone has exactly one manager, every manager knows their direct reports, and people are clustered into sensible teams. "Clarity and simplicity are important -- I wouldn't try to innovate on management structure" [1].

A clear mission and clear goals reduce the burden of management. The better the job a founder does with these, the less management feels overbearing [1].

### Common Management Failures

**Avoiding senior hires**: In the early days, hiring senior people is usually a mistake. As the company scales, adding executives who have built companies before becomes valuable [1].

**Not delegating**: Many founders take on too much personally. This works temporarily but creates a breaking point as the company grows. Delegation is essential [1].

**Delegating poorly**: Some founders delegate grunt work but retain all decisions. This fails for scalability and for making employees effective. Instead, empower employees to own decision processes. "Let them know how you think about a problem, but let them own the decision process" [1]. Delegate the things you do not want to do. Retain involvement in the one thing you care most about [1].

**Personal disorganization**: Developing a system to track tasks and follow-ups is not critical when focused purely on product, but becomes crucial as the founder becomes a manager [1].

**Undercommunicating**: After a certain point, one-on-one conversations with everyone are impossible. The solution: learn to communicate in clear, succinct writing and share it with everyone. "It's one of the highest leverage things you can do, and it scales to thousands of people" [1]. All-hands meetings at least monthly remain important, as do one-on-ones with direct reports [1].

### The Alignment Test

Altman describes a classic diagnostic: "If I asked 10 employees what the top three goals are for the company, would they say the same thing?" Founders always say yes. When Altman actually asks the employees, no two say the same thing. Founders consistently overestimate how effectively they communicate their ideas. Reiterating the roadmap and goals must become habitual [1].

## HR

Most founders ignore HR early, but continuing to ignore it as the company scales is a major mistake. Good HR means three things: a clear management structure, a way for people to raise workplace concerns, and pathways for career evolution [1].

### Performance and Compensation

Performance feedback should happen frequently, even if light. People benefit from regularly hearing how they are doing [1].

Compensation must be structured with bands to keep things fair. "People talk, and they will eventually find out co-workers' compensation levels. If it's all over the place, it can be a complete disaster" [1].

### Equity

YC company data suggests that the most successful companies give out significant equity. Altman advises planning to give away 3-5% of the company each year for ten years from founding. This includes [refresher grants](https://blog.wealthfront.com/how-long-should-you-stay-at-your-job/) for employees approaching vesting cliffs. "You never want an employee in a place where they start thinking about leaving because they've vested three out of their four years. Always stay in front of people's vesting schedules" [1].

### Hiring Process and Diversity

At the 12-24 month mark with [[Product-Market Fit]] achieved, hire a full-time recruiter. Before that is premature. Additional practices [1]:
- **Build a diverse team**: Unity of vision, diversity of backgrounds. Myopic culture is less effective.
- **Announce offers internally**: Up to a few hundred employees, share potential job offers on an internal mailing list. Often someone will know something relevant about the candidate.
- **Structure onboarding**: Have a program so every new employee's first week is planned.
- **Build paths for early employees**: The original 10-15 employees may not fit new executive roles but are loved by peers and highly productive. Proactively discuss their career paths.

### Burnout Monitoring

Building a company is a marathon. Working 100-hour weeks is not sustainable. Monitor the team for burnout, ensure people take vacations, have new challenges, and do new things [1].

## Company Productivity

Building a company that can innovate repeatedly is the hardest thing in business. Most companies do one great thing then stop. Alignment is the key lever -- companies become unproductive when people are not on the same page or do not understand priorities [1].

The solution is a clear mission, roadmap, and goals. Everyone should know the roadmap for the next 3-6 months or a year. Transparent communication through weekly management meetings with direct reports and monthly all-hands meetings is critical [1].

### Longer-Term Productivity Practices

**Offsites**: Take the best people outside the normal workspace for a weekend of big-picture thinking [1].

**Legal document organization**: Assign someone to collect every agreement the company has signed. This saves headaches later [1].

**FP&A**: Building a great financial model of the business enables optimization that most founders miss. Most companies do not hire for this until they have a few hundred employees, but doing it sooner is worthwhile [1].

**Dedicated fundraiser**: Hiring a full-time fundraiser after the B round, whose job is to prepare for the C round, yields better results and less dilution than hiring an investment banker later [1].

**Unit economics**: Figure out how to make more money from each user than you spend, sooner rather than later. Most great companies historically had good unit economics soon after monetization, even if the company as a whole lost money. "It can be tempting to paper over a problem with the business by spending more money instead of fixing the product or service, but this is a major trap" [1].

**Runway management**: Even with great unit economics, never get down to just a couple months of cash in the bank [1].

## Founder Psychology

As the company succeeds, the highs get higher but the lows keep getting lower. People start rooting against the founder. Journalists and internet critics find ways to pick apart the work. Learning to ignore critics early is important [1].

Altman recommends making a genuine long-term commitment: "Have a strategy that assumes you will work on your company for the next 10 years -- it can be an extreme advantage that few founders leverage" [1].

Taking vacations is non-negotiable. Founders who go three or four years without a real vacation will burn out [1].

Focus must be maintained. It is what made the company successful in the first place. Common focus-killers include burnout and talking to potential acquirers [1].

## Marketing and PR

Ignore marketing and PR for a long time. "Press will not make your startup." But once the product is working, shift to caring about it a little [1].

Critical rules:
- Never outsource key messaging to a PR firm or head of marketing. The founder must figure out the company's message [1].
- Pick three or four journalists to develop close relationships with. "No journalist wants to talk to a PR firm; they're much happier to hear from the founder" [1].

## Dealmaking

Business development can start to matter at 12-24 months, assuming a great product exists. Key principles [1]:
- Develop personal connections with counterparts. No one wants to feel used transactionally.
- Set up competitive situations. This is basic negotiation -- you get good terms when you have alternatives and stay persistent.
- Ask for what you want. "Most of the time you won't get laughed out of the room, and you might actually get it" [1].

## References

1. [Later Stage Advice for Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4q-later-stage-advice-for-startups) -- [[Sam Altman]]

title: Convincing Investors
body:
# Convincing Investors

Convincing investors is not a performance problem. It is a truth problem. [[Paul Graham]] argues that inexperienced founders make the same mistake as people who hurt themselves lifting heavy things: they try to lift with their back instead of their legs. Founders try to convince with their pitch rather than letting the startup do the work [1]. The correct approach is to first understand why the startup is worth investing in, then explain that clearly.

## What Investors Are Looking For

Investors seek startups that will be very successful. Because startup outcomes follow a power law with a steep curve, most investors treat "big success" as binary. The conventional wisdom is that roughly 15 startups per year become the big successes, and investors are interested in a founder only if there is some chance, however small, of being one of those 15 [1].

Three things make a startup look like a sufficiently good bet [1]:

1. **Formidable founders**
2. **A promising market**
3. **Some evidence of success so far**

## Formidable Founders

Most investors decide within the first few minutes whether a founder seems like a winner or a loser, and once that opinion is set, it is hard to change [1]. If investors think you are a winner, they focus on reasons to invest. If not, they focus on reasons not to. The same startup characteristic — say, a rich market with a slow sales cycle — gets framed positively or negatively depending on this initial impression [1].

Graham defines "formidable" as someone who seems like they will get what they want, regardless of obstacles. It is close to "confident" but with the added dimension of being justifiably confident [1].

The way to seem formidable as an inexperienced founder is to stick to the truth. Most people can seem confident saying "one plus one is two" because they know it is true. The same principle scales to more impressive claims, as long as the founder has first convinced themselves [1]. Graham writes:

> "Convince yourself that your startup is worth investing in, and then when you explain this to investors they'll believe you." [1]

This requires being a domain expert. Investors can tell quickly whether founders truly understand their market by how they answer questions. A non-expert who is merely enthusiastic will register as an instance of the Dunning-Kruger effect [1].

### What YC Looks For

The YC interview is not a pitch — it is a question-and-answer session. Interviews are 10 minutes long (7 minutes would suffice in practice, but 10 is socially convenient) [2]. The partners need random access to the founders' knowledge, not a sequential presentation [2].

Graham states that if he had to decide whether to fund startups based on a single question, it would be: "How do you know people want this?" [2]. The most convincing answer is "because we and our friends want it," especially when followed by evidence of a crude prototype spreading by word of mouth [2].

What YC looks for above all is founders who understand some group of users and can make what they want. The motto is "Make something people want" [2]. The best thing a founder can do in a YC interview is teach the partners about their users [2].

The worst advice Graham has heard about YC interviews is to take control and deliver a prepared message. When founders do this, it wastes the 10 minutes on prefabricated pitch material instead of genuine answers [2].

### Honesty Over Polish

When asked what could go wrong, the worst answer is "nothing." That convinces partners the founder is either a fool or a liar. Far better to go into detail about risks — that is what experts do [2]. The same principle applies to competitors. Competitors rarely kill startups (poor execution does), but founders should know who their competitors are and discuss strengths and weaknesses candidly [2].

The partners do not expect a perfect idea at seed stage. They expect promising hypotheses. But they do expect founders to be thoughtful and honest [2].

## The Market

Founders think of startups as ideas; investors think of them as markets [1]. The total addressable market (TAM) is the number of potential customers multiplied by average annual spend. The market has to be big, but it does not have to be big yet. Graham notes that it is often better to start in a small market that will turn into a big one, or from which the founder can move into a big one [1].

The standard of proof varies by company age. A three-month-old company at Demo Day only needs to be a promising experiment. A two-year-old company raising a Series A needs to show the experiment worked [1]. At three months, a growth graph is evidence the founders are effective. At two years, it must be evidence of a promising market and a company tuned to exploit it [1].

### The "Why Now?" Question

Every company that gets really big is "lucky" in the sense that growth comes from riding an external wave. To make a convincing case, founders must identify a specific trend they will benefit from. The question "why now?" is critical: if it is such a great idea, why hasn't someone else done it? The ideal answer is that it only recently became a good idea because something changed, and no one else has noticed yet [1].

Microsoft illustrates this. Selling Basic interpreters was not going to produce huge growth, but it positioned Microsoft to expand up the stack as microcomputers grew powerful enough to support more software. Microcomputers turned out to be a bigger wave than even the most optimistic observers predicted in 1975 [1].

## Handling Rejection

Getting rejected is common — even for the best companies. Some VCs would have rejected Microsoft. Some rejected Google [1]. The most common question from investors is "who else is investing?" which puts rejected founders in an awkward position [1].

Graham advises against trying to bluff about other investors' interest. It is the most common lie told to investors, and most founders cannot pull it off [1]. The better approach is to tackle the problem head-on: explain why investors rejected you and why they are mistaken. Experienced investors know that the best ideas are also the scariest, and they all know about the VCs who rejected Google [1].

This strategy works best with the best investors, who are both hard to bluff and already believe most other investors are conventional-minded. Raising money is not like college admissions — a startup rejected by mediocre VCs may still be funded by top ones [1]. Graham recounts that Dropbox was part of a YC Boston batch. All the Boston investors passed. A couple weeks later, Dropbox raised a Series A from Sequoia [1].

## The Three-Step Recipe

Graham distills the approach for founders who are not naturally formidable [1]:

1. Make something worth investing in.
2. Understand why it is worth investing in.
3. Explain that clearly to investors.

Use the same matter-of-fact language used to convince yourself. Conciseness is a test many investors explicitly use, reasoning correctly that founders who cannot explain plans concisely do not really understand them. Alfred Lin emphasizes this is doubly important because the explanation must work at one remove — it must convince not just the partner you speak with, but that partner when re-telling it to colleagues [1].

## Authenticity and Motivation

Founders' motives are usually mixed: money, status, genuine interest in the problem, and unwillingness to work for someone else. The last two are more powerful motivators than the first two [2]. Founders doing it just for money will take the first large acquisition offer. Founders doing it just for status will discover less painful ways to seem cool [2].

The defining quality of founders who build billion-dollar companies is that they are genuinely interested in what they are building. The company is their project. Every billionaire founder could have stopped sooner — gotten acquired or hired someone else to run the company. They kept working because there was nothing else they would rather do [2].

Graham writes:

> "The most reliable way to become a billionaire is to start a company that grows fast, and the way to grow fast is to make what users want." [2]

YC rejects founders whose approach is to exploit people. Bad people make bad founders: their exploitation begins with co-founders (destroying the company's foundation), then moves to users (the hardest people to fool), and the best outcome is a small acquisition before the deception collapses [2].

## References

1. [How to Convince Investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/98-how-to-convince-investors) — [[Paul Graham]] (August 2013)
2. [Billionaires Build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build) — [[Paul Graham]] (December 2020)

title: Doing Things That Don't Scale
body:
# Doing Things That Don't Scale

One of the most common types of advice given at Y Combinator is to do things that don't scale [1]. [[Paul Graham]] wrote the definitive essay on this in July 2013, arguing that startups take off because the founders make them take off. The belief that startups either take off or don't -- that you build something, make it available, and either people beat a path to your door or the market doesn't exist -- is wrong [1].

"Actually startups take off because the founders make them take off. There may be a handful that just grew by themselves, but usually it takes some sort of push to get them going. A good metaphor would be the cranks that car engines had before they got electric starters. Once the engine was going, it would keep going, but there was a separate and laborious process to get it going." [1]

## Recruit Users Manually

The most common unscalable thing founders must do at the start is recruit users manually. Nearly all startups have to do this. You cannot wait for users to come to you [1].

Stripe is the canonical example. Despite solving an urgent problem -- one where you might think users would find them on their own -- the Collison brothers were famous within YC for aggressive early user acquisition [1]. They invented what YC calls the "Collison installation": when anyone agreed to try Stripe, instead of saying "Great, we'll send you a link," the Collisons would say "Right then, give me your laptop" and set them up on the spot [1].

Two reasons founders resist manual recruitment [1]:
1. **Shyness and laziness.** They would rather sit at home writing code than go out and talk to strangers and probably be rejected by most of them. But for a startup to succeed, at least one founder (usually the CEO) will have to spend a lot of time on sales and marketing.
2. **The numbers seem too small.** Founders think this cannot be how the big, famous startups got started. They underestimate compound growth. If you have 100 users and grow 10% a week, after a year you have 14,000 users; after two years, 2 million [1].

Airbnb is another classic example. In the early days, Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia went door to door in New York, recruiting new users and helping existing ones improve their listings. Graham recalls them always showing up to Tuesday YC dinners with rolly bags because they had just flown back from somewhere [1].

## The Fragility of Early Startups

Almost all startups are fragile initially [1]. Airbnb now seems like an unstoppable juggernaut, but early on it was so fragile that about 30 days of going out and engaging in person with users made the difference between success and failure [1].

Inexperienced founders, investors, reporters, and know-it-alls on forums unconsciously judge larval startups by the standards of established ones. "They're like someone looking at a newborn baby and concluding 'there's no way this tiny creature could ever accomplish anything'" [1].

The right question about an early-stage startup: not "is this company taking over the world?" but "how big could this company get if the founders did the right things?" [1]. Even Bill Gates underestimated Microsoft -- he returned to Harvard for the fall semester after starting the company [1]. Microsoft did not seem impressive when it was just a couple of guys in Albuquerque writing Basic interpreters for a market of a few thousand hobbyists [1].

## Delight Your Users

Founders should take extraordinary measures not just to acquire users but to make them happy [1]. Wufoo sent each new user a hand-written thank you note, for as long as they could (which turned out to be surprisingly long) [1].

Three reasons founders do not focus enough on delighting individual users [1]:

1. **Engineering training.** Engineers are trained to build robust and elegant things, not to be slavishly attentive to individual users. But at an early-stage startup, the founder is Kirk, not Scotty.
2. **Worry about scalability.** In their current state, they have nothing to lose. "See if you can make it happen" -- having too many users to personally delight would be a great problem to have.
3. **Low standards from experience.** Their standards for customer service have been set by big companies. Tim Cook does not send you a hand-written note after you buy a laptop. He cannot. But a small startup can, and that is an advantage of being small.

Graham states: "I have never once seen a startup lured down a blind alley by trying too hard to make their initial users happy" [1].

## Insanely Great Experience, Not Product

Steve Jobs's phrase "insanely great" applied to early startups means something different than it did at an established Apple [1]. For a larval startup, it is not the product that should be insanely great, but the experience of being your user. The product is just one component. You can and should give users an insanely great experience with an early, incomplete, buggy product, if you make up the difference with attentiveness [1].

This over-engagement is not just a permissible technique for getting growth rolling. For most successful startups it is a necessary part of the feedback loop that makes the product good [1]. The feedback from engaging directly with your earliest users will be the best you ever get. When you are big enough to resort to focus groups, you will wish you could go over to users' homes and offices like you did when there were only a handful of them [1].

## The Contained Fire Strategy

Sometimes the right unscalable trick is to focus on a deliberately narrow market. "It's like keeping a fire contained at first to get it really hot before adding more logs" [1].

Facebook did this. At first it was just for Harvard students -- a potential market of a few thousand people. But because they felt it was really for them, a critical mass signed up. When Graham interviewed Mark Zuckerberg at Startup School, Zuckerberg said that while it was a lot of work creating course lists for each school, doing that made students feel the site was their natural home [1].

Among companies, the best early adopters are usually other startups. They are more open to new things by nature and because, having just been started, they haven't made all their choices yet. When they succeed they grow fast, and you with them [1].

## Pulling a Meraki: Hardware Startups

For hardware startups, there is a variant called "pulling a Meraki" [1]. The Meraki founders (Robert Morris's grad students) got started by assembling their routers themselves. Hardware startups face a catch-22: the minimum order for a factory production run is usually several hundred thousand dollars, but without a product you cannot generate the growth to raise that money [1].

Pebble assembled the first several hundred watches themselves. If they had not, they probably would not have sold $10 million worth on Kickstarter [1]. Eric Migicovsky said one of the things he learned was "how valuable it was to source good screws" [1].

## The Consulting Technique

For B2B startups, Graham sometimes advises taking over-engagement to an extreme: pick a single user and act as if you were consultants building something just for that one user [1]. The initial user serves as the form for your mold. Keep tweaking till you fit their needs perfectly, and you will usually find you have made something other users want too [1].

At Viaweb, when merchants would not use the software themselves, Graham and Morris offered to make stores for them. "We felt pretty lame at the time. Instead of organizing big strategic e-commerce partnerships, we were trying to sell luggage and pens and men's shirts" [1]. But it was exactly the right thing because it taught them how merchants would use the software. The feedback loop was sometimes near-instantaneous: in the middle of building a merchant's site, Graham would find he needed a feature they did not have, spend a couple hours implementing it, and resume building the site [1].

## Being Your Own Software

A more extreme variant: when you only have a small number of users, you can sometimes do by hand things you plan to automate later [1]. This lets you launch faster, and when you finally automate yourself out of the loop, you know exactly what to build because you have muscle memory from doing it yourself.

Stripe delivered "instant" merchant accounts to its first users by having the founders manually sign them up for traditional merchant accounts behind the scenes [1].

## Big Launches Usually Don't Work

Graham explicitly warns against the "Big Launch" -- launching simultaneously in 8 publications with embargoes on a Tuesday [1]. Think of successful startups: how many of their launches do you remember? All you need from a launch is some initial core of users. How well you are doing a few months later will depend more on how happy you made those users than how many there were [1].

Partnerships also usually do not work as a way to get growth started. "Six months later they're all saying the same thing: that was way more work than we expected, and we ended up getting practically nothing out of it" [1].

## Startup Ideas as Vectors

Graham proposes thinking of startup ideas not as scalars but as pairs: what you are going to build, plus the unscalable thing you are going to do initially to get the company going [1]. In most cases the second component is: recruit users manually and give them an overwhelmingly good experience [1].

In the best case, both components of the vector contribute to the company's DNA. The unscalable things you do to get started are not merely a necessary evil but change the company permanently for the better [1]. If you have to be aggressive about user acquisition when you are small, you will probably still be aggressive when you are big. If you have to work hard to delight users when you only have a handful, you will keep doing it when you have a lot [1].

## Connection to Other Principles

The essay on [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]] connects directly to Graham's essay on [[Default Alive or Default Dead]]. In that essay, he argues that instead of hiring to boost growth, startups should seek growth "by doing things that don't scale, or by redesigning the product in the way only founders can" [3]. Airbnb waited 4 months after raising money at the end of YC before hiring their first employee. The founders were terribly overworked, but they were overworked evolving Airbnb into the astonishingly successful organism it became [3].

Graham's earlier essay on the hardest lessons also reinforces this: "Release early" and "keep pumping out features" -- get a version 1 out fast, then improve it based on users' reactions [2]. Wufoo released their form-builder before the underlying database, getting 83,000 people to sit in the driver's seat. Linux users complained about Flash, so Wufoo rewrote their software. If they had waited to release everything at once, they would not have discovered the problem until it was deeply wired in [2].

## References

1. [Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale) -- [[Paul Graham]] (July 2013)
2. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2006)
3. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2015)

title: Product-Market Fit
body:
# Product-Market Fit

Product-market fit describes the state where a startup's product satisfies strong market demand. It is one of the most referenced concepts in the YC canon, cited across 11+ articles in this wiki as the prerequisite for nearly every subsequent startup activity -- from [[Growth]] investment to [[Series A Fundraising]].

## The Concept

Product-market fit is the moment when a product resonates so strongly with its target market that demand begins to outpace the company's ability to supply. [[Sam Altman]] frames it as the precondition for growth: founders who focus on growth before building something people love are unlikely to succeed. Facebook had a product people talked about obsessively from the start; Airbnb slogged for 1,000 days before discovering how to make their product loved [1].

[[Paul Graham]] approaches the concept from the other direction: "In a sense there's just one mistake that kills startups: not making something users want" [2]. The organic method of finding PMF is to live in the future and build what is missing, rather than generating ideas on demand [3].

## Signals of Product-Market Fit

Several speakers describe how to recognize PMF:

Altman's test: "When they actively recommend your product to friends, you're in the right place." Net Promoter Score can be a useful measurement [1].

[[Anu Hariharan]] provides quantitative benchmarks through retention metrics. If the retention curve flattens and runs parallel to the x-axis after an initial dip, and the long-term retention rate is in line with vertical benchmarks, the product has achieved fit [4]. See [[Growth]] for specific retention benchmarks by vertical.

[[Emmett Shear]] frames it through feature prioritization: once PMF is established, the question shifts from "does anyone want this?" to "what should we build next?" -- a qualitatively different problem [5].

## PMF and Growth

The relationship between PMF and growth is sequential, not simultaneous. The consensus across multiple speakers is that growth investment before PMF is wasteful and dangerous. Hariharan's growth team guide is explicit: do not build a growth team before you have proven Retention [4]. Altman warns that growing on a mediocre product creates a "leaky bucket" -- users come in the door but do not stay [1].

Once PMF is achieved, users become "a free marketing and sales force." Word-of-mouth growth is powerful even for enterprise and hard tech companies [1].

## PMF and Fundraising

PMF is increasingly the threshold for [[Series A Fundraising]]. Compared to the seed round, it is harder to raise a Series A on promise alone -- investors want to see results that demonstrate the product works [6]. The traction slides in a Series A deck are fundamentally evidence of PMF.

## References

1. [Before Growing Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4p-before-growing-your-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]]
2. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
3. [How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas) -- [[Paul Graham]] (November 2012)
4. [How to Set Up, Hire, and Scale a Growth Strategy and Team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/59-how-to-set-up-hire-and-scale-a-growth-strategy-and-team) -- [[Anu Hariharan]], Gustaf Alströmer
5. [How to Prioritize Features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features) -- [[Emmett Shear]]
6. [How to Build a Great Series A Pitch and Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck) -- Janelle Tam

title: Starting Young
body:
# Starting Young

The question of when to start a startup -- and particularly whether to start one in or immediately after college -- is one [[Paul Graham]] has revisited multiple times, with his position evolving from encouragement (2005-2007) to more nuanced caution (2014). The tension between the advantages of youth and the dangers of starting too early runs through several of his most important essays.

## The Sweet Spot: Mid-Twenties

Graham's considered view, expressed in his 2006 student guide, is that the sweet spot for startup founders is the mid-twenties [1]. The question is what advantages 25-year-olds have over 21-year-olds, and why the answer is not older still.

**Advantages of young founders [1]:**

1. **Stamina.** Long hours are universal in successful startups. Younger founders need this even more because they are probably less efficient than they will be later.

2. **Poverty.** This sounds counterintuitive, but poverty means you can live cheaply, which is critically important because nearly every startup that fails does so by running out of money. A low burn rate gives more opportunity to recover from mistakes. "The guys with kids and mortgages are at a real disadvantage" [1]. Even more important than living cheaply is thinking cheaply. The Apple II was popular partly because Woz designed it for himself and couldn't afford anything more [1].

3. **Rootlessness.** Young people are more mobile -- no house, less stuff, fewer serious relationships. Many startups involve someone moving, and a startup hub matters enormously. Houston, Chicago, and Miami have microscopically small numbers of successful startups per capita [1].

4. **Colleagues.** The number one question at YC is "Where can I find a cofounder?" At 30, your school friends have either lost touch or are tied down by jobs. At a good college, you are concentrated with ambitious, technical people more than you will ever be again [1]. "Co-founders really should be people you already know. And by far the best place to meet them is school" [1]. Google, Yahoo, and Microsoft were all founded by people who met in school [1].

5. **Ignorance (innocence).** Jessica Livingston's interviews with startup founders revealed a pattern: "One after another said that if they'd known how hard it would be, they would have been too intimidated to start" [1]. Steve Wozniak put it strongly: "All the best things that I did at Apple came from (a) not having money and (b) not having done it before, ever" [1].

**Disadvantages of young founders [1]:**

The core problem is that young founders build things that look like class projects -- lacking (1) an iterative definition of a real problem and (2) market-level intensity [1]. See [[Early-Stage Mistakes]] for the full analysis.

The 24-year-old's real advantage over the 20-year-old: "they know what they're trying to avoid" [1]. To the average undergrad, getting rich means buying Ferraris. To someone who has learned about the relationship between money and work, it means opting out of the brutal equation governing 99.9% of people's lives. "Someone who gets this will work much harder at making a startup succeed -- with the proverbial energy of a drowning man" [1].

## The Excuses for Not Starting

Graham's 2007 essay systematically dismantles 16 common reasons people give for not starting a startup [2]. His goal is explicitly to increase self-confidence, but with the constraint that he is motivated to be honest -- encouraging people who shouldn't start startups makes his own life worse [2].

**Excuses that are mostly bogus:**

- **Too young.** The most successful founder YC had funded at the time, Sam Altman, was 19. But Altman is an outlier: "he seemed like he had a 40-year-old inside him" [2]. The real test: whether you have crossed the adult threshold, not your chronological age.

- **Too inexperienced.** "The best way to get experience if you're 21 is to start a startup" [2]. The Kiko founders started right out of college, made a lot of mistakes, and by the time YC funded their second startup a year later, they had become "extremely formidable" [2]. Getting a normal job may actually make you less able to start a startup by "turning you into a tame animal who thinks he needs an office to work in and a product manager to tell him what software to write" [2].

- **Not smart enough.** "If you're smart enough to worry that you might not be smart enough to start a startup, you probably are" [2]. Starting a startup does not require exceptional intelligence. Enterprise software companies are sales companies, and sales depends mostly on effort [2].

- **Know nothing about business.** The coefficient of this variable should be zero. "You don't need to know anything about business to start a startup. The initial focus should be the product" [2]. Pretty much 100% of startups that make something popular manage to make money from it. Acquirers buy strategic value, meaning something people want [2].

- **No idea.** In the average YC startup, 70% of the idea is new at the end of the first three months, sometimes 100%. YC is "so sure the founders are more important than the initial idea" that they accept applications with no idea at all [2].

- **No room for more startups.** There is no economic limit on the number of startups. "Startups make wealth, which means they make things people want, and if there's a limit on the number of things people want, we are nowhere near it. I still don't even have a flying car" [2].

- **Fear of uncertainty.** "If you start a startup, it will probably fail. Seriously, though, this is not a bad way to think about the whole experience." No employer will hold it against you. "In America, companies, like practically everything else, are disposable" [2].

**Excuses that are real:**

- **Not determined enough.** Determination is probably the single best predictor of success. But most hackers underestimate their own determination [2].

- **No cofounder.** "A startup is too much for one person to bear." All investors, without exception, are more likely to fund you with a cofounder. If you don't have one, get one -- "It's more important than anything else" [2].

- **Family to support.** Graham won't advise anyone with a family to start a startup. An alternative: start a consulting business and gradually turn it into a product business, or join an existing startup as an early employee [2].

- **Not ready for commitment.** If a startup succeeds, it will consume at least three or four years. This is a legitimate reason to wait, though Graham notes you will probably spend just as long at a regular job with less spare time than you expect [2].

## The 2014 Recalibration

By October 2014, Graham had shifted to more cautionary advice for students [3]. His Stanford lecture argues explicitly: "Do not start a startup in college" [3].

The reasoning: starting a startup at 20 is "like a brutally fast depth-first search. Most people should still be searching breadth-first at 20" [3]. You can do things in your early 20s that you cannot do before or after -- "plunge deeply into projects on a whim and travel super cheaply with no sense of a deadline" [3].

Mark Zuckerberg will never get to bum around a foreign country. "Success has taken a lot of the serendipity out of his life. Facebook is running him as much as he's running Facebook" [3].

The key insight: there is not even a tradeoff. You are not sacrificing anything by waiting, because you are more likely to succeed if you do [3]. The usual way startups take off is for founders to make them take off (see [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]]), and "it's gratuitously stupid to do that at 20" [3].

What students should do instead: "just learn" [3]. Become good at things that matter. Work on problems that interest you. The optimal preparation is not a vocational version of college focused on entrepreneurship but "the classic version of college as education for its own sake" [3].

Graham flags university entrepreneurship programs as misguided. "They go out of their way to ensure their students are well supplied with contraceptives, and yet they're setting up entrepreneurship programs and startup incubators left and right" [3]. The university cannot teach the essential thing -- understanding user needs -- because that requires actually starting the company, at which point you are no longer really a student [3].

## The Google Trap

Graham observes that while Google seems like the ideal place to work, "zero startups come out of there. In that respect it's a black hole" [1]. People like working there too much to leave. If you hope to start a startup one day, a pleasant employer can lull you into staying indefinitely even if leaving would be a net win. You need "activation energy" to start a startup, and a comfortable job suppresses it [1].

The best place to work if you want to start a startup is probably a startup. It is the right sort of experience, and one way or another it will be over quickly: you will either end up rich, or the startup will get acquired and start to suck, or it will blow up and you will be free [1].

## The Default Shift

Graham sees the shift from employment as default to startups as an option in historical terms. "Getting a job" is only about a hundred years old as the default way to make a living -- before that it was farming [2]. The shift from farming to manufacturing would have seemed insanely risky to medieval serfs [2].

"How grim it would be to commute every day to a cubicle in some soulless office complex, and be told what to do by someone you had to acknowledge as a boss -- someone who could call you into their office and say 'take a seat,' and you'd sit! Imagine having to ask permission to release software to users" [2].

Graham believes startups may represent another such historic shift in how wealth is created [2].

## References

1. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
2. [Why to Not Not Start a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (March 2007)
3. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2014)
4. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
5. [Why Smart People Have Bad Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/91-why-smart-people-have-bad-ideas) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2005)

title: Startup Management
body:
# Startup Management

Most startup founders begin managing teams with zero management experience. As Aaron Epstein, founder of Creative Market, describes it: "One of the most difficult things about starting a company is that you have to create both a product that people love and a company where people want to work at the same time" [1]. This article compiles practical management advice for founders who are building and leading teams for the first time.

## The Core Tension

The dual mandate of startups creates a fundamental challenge: "Great people build great products, and great people won't tolerate a bad environment for very long" [1]. The commonly cited observation that "people leave managers, not companies" applies with particular force to startups, where many first-time founders "raise a bunch of money and start building a team without any management experience at all" [1].

## Delegation and Scaling

Delegation is the foundational management skill for founders. Epstein frames it as a necessary evolution: "As a founder you're used to doing it all, and it can be scary to shed responsibilities you've always owned, but you can't scale yourself and focus on the highest leverage opportunities until you get things off your plate" [1].

The practical signal for when to delegate: "If you think you're going to have to work especially late or can't get to the things you need to in a given day, take that as a signal to delegate to your team" [1].

The flip side of delegation is growth opportunity creation. Great employees often value personal growth more than compensation: "Don't hoard [new responsibilities] all for yourself" [1]. This connects to the broader principle from [[Startup Leadership]] that a CEO's role transitions from Doer-in-Chief to Company-Builder-in-Chief.

## Investing in Star Employees

Epstein draws an analogy to customer retention: "Similar to how it's much easier to retain an existing customer than to acquire a new one, it's much easier to keep an existing star employee happy than find a new star" [1]. The advice is to go "above and beyond" to make stars feel valued, appreciated, and rewarded.

## Setting Standards and Accountability

### High Bar

"Great people want to do big, meaningful things. Push your team with aggressive goals, and you might be surprised what they will accomplish" [1].

### Deadlines and Follow-Through

When assigning tasks, always ask "by when?" and add a calendar note to follow up [1]. When standards or deadlines are not met, give direct feedback to change future behavior.

### Verbalizing Feedback

Epstein emphasizes the power of simply stating feelings: "'I'm disappointed in this work' is extremely powerful in helping people directly understand where you stand. People aren't mind-readers, so rather than just jumping into solutions when giving feedback, state your feelings out loud" [1].

## Leading by Example

"Teams embody the characteristics of their leaders, and the things you care about will be the things your team cares about" [1]. If a founder pays attention to details, the team will learn to do the same. This extends to credit and blame: "Use 'I' when talking about a screw-up, and use 'we' (or better yet, specific contributors' names) when talking about successes" [1].

## Communication

### Sharing the Big Picture

Employees need to understand how their work fits into the company's larger goals. "It's easy to take it for granted that your team is clear on this, but this is one of those things where you want to constantly make that connection" [1]. This reinforces the [[Mission-to-Metrics Framework]] that [[Ali Rowghani]] describes as one of a CEO's three non-delegable tasks.

### Repetition

"You should feel like you sound like a broken record about the things that are important to your company -- your mission, your vision, your KPIs, etc." [1]. The test for adequate repetition: "every single person on your team will be able to explain them to a complete stranger exactly how you would've said it" [1].

## Hiring

### Hire Slow, Fire Fast

"It's the thing everyone says and few have the discipline to do" [1]. The reasoning: "Great people want to work with other great people, so resist the temptation to fill open positions with mediocre candidates, and don't settle. You should be thrilled about each new hire" [1].

### Hire for Hunger

Skills can be learned; passion cannot be manufactured. "Passion for your mission, business, and team will do more to drive an employee to make a big, long-term impact than any specific skills they may have" [1].

### Onboarding

"It's unfair to expect even the best people to hit the ground running on day one without being properly onboarded" [1]. Managers must invest early in setting expectations, showing the ropes, teaching the business, and providing clear direction.

### Earning Trust

New employees should not be given full autonomy immediately: "Make them prove themselves first by working more closely with them and frequently reviewing their work until they've earned your full trust" [1].

## Adapting Management Style to Individuals

Epstein's favorite management interview question is "How would you describe your management style?" and "the best possible answer is 'it depends on the employee'" [1]. Each person has a unique combination of experience, motivations, and personality. The assumption that everyone is self-motivated like the founder is a common mistake: "Get curious to figure out what drives and motivates each individual on your team, rather than assuming everyone's like you" [1].

## Decision-Making

### Including the Team

"If you've hired great people, you'd be crazy not to include them in important decisions" [1]. The key is defining the process upfront: will the decision be made by committee, delegated to someone, or made by the founder after gathering input? "Even if your team disagrees with the final call, by including them in the process they will better understand the decision, feel like their opinion was valued, and be able to get on board" [1].

### Showing Your Work

"Explain your decision-making process" [1]. When taking controversial actions or making difficult decisions, explaining the rationale earns buy-in and teaches the team how to make similar decisions.

### Admitting Uncertainty

"There's nothing wrong with saying you'll need some time to think or learn more before making an important decision" [1].

## Healthy Tension

"Differing perspectives help make teams and products better, so a little professional tension can be really valuable" [1]. The limit: tension must be net-positive. If it forces decisions to stall or shifts from professional to personal, "you need to take action to eliminate it quickly before it drags down the team or blows up" [1].

## One-on-Ones

"Have consistent 1-on-1s every week" [1]. These meetings are for the employee's benefit, so they should drive the agenda. The manager's role is to ask open-ended questions: "How are things going?", "How are you feeling?", "What's your opinion on X?" Prioritizing these meetings and refusing to cancel them "will send a signal to your team that you care about their happiness" [1].

## The Maker-to-Manager Transition

As organizations grow, the best individual contributors are promoted to managers. Epstein warns that "just because someone is an outstanding individual contributor doesn't mean they'll be an outstanding manager right off the bat, because it requires a completely different set of skills and experience" [1].

The common failure mode: new managers try to do both their old maker work and their new management duties, "spending 75% effort on making and 75% effort on managing -- a 150% workload resulting in less than 100% output in each area" [1]. The solution is to "put structure in place to allow them to focus on being a manager first and foremost, and take maker duties off of their plate" [1].

## Career Development

"Every 6 months, ask your employees where they want to be in their career in the next 2-3 years. Work with them to put together a plan, help them get the skills and experience they need, and guide them on a course to get there" [1].

## References

1. [Advice for New Managers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2s-advice-for-new-managers) — Aaron Epstein

title: Seed Fundraising
body:
# Seed Fundraising

Seed fundraising is the process by which startup founders raise initial outside capital to fund operations, growth, and product development. Most startups require external capital because the cost of reaching profitability exceeds what founders and their personal networks can finance [1]. Geoff Ralston, drawing on his experience at YC, describes fundraising as "brutal" but a path "almost all companies and founders must walk" [1]. This article covers the complete landscape of seed fundraising: when to raise, how much to raise, the mechanics of financing instruments, how to build a pitch deck, and how to close investors.

## When to Raise

Investors write checks when three conditions align: the idea is compelling, the founding team appears capable of executing, and the opportunity is real and sufficiently large [1]. For most founders, this requires having a product with measurable traction. Ralston specifies that a growth rate of 10% per week for several weeks is "impressive" and sufficient to attract investor interest [1]. For founders who lack traction, the advice is direct: "work on your product and talk to your users" [1].

The general principle is that when you can raise money, you should. Cash provides competitive advantage in hiring, marketing, sales, and public relations [1].

## How Much to Raise

The ideal amount is enough to reach profitability, eliminating the need for future rounds entirely [1]. When that is not realistic (as with hardware companies or capital-intensive businesses), the target should be enough to reach the next "fundable" milestone, typically 12 to 18 months away [1].

Ralston offers a concrete formula for calculating the amount: an engineer costs approximately $15,000 per month all-in. For 18 months of operations with five engineers, the calculation is 15k x 5 x 18 = $1.35 million [1]. Most seed rounds cluster around $600,000, though rounds have been increasing in size [1].

On dilution, founders should aim to give up no more than 10% of the company in a seed round, though most rounds require up to 20% dilution. Exceeding 25% dilution should be avoided [1]. The amount raised must be tied to a believable plan, and founders should prepare multiple scenarios showing different growth rates at different funding levels [1].

Seed valuations tend to range from $2 million to $10 million, but Ralston cautions against over-optimizing on valuation: "the goal is not to achieve the best valuation, nor does a high valuation increase your likelihood of success" [1].

## Financing Instruments

### SAFEs (Simple Agreement for Future Equity)

The SAFE has almost completely replaced convertible debt at YC [1]. A SAFE has no interest rate, no maturity date, and no repayment requirement. The negotiable terms are simply the investment amount, the valuation cap, and the discount (if any) [1]. SAFEs are popular because closing mechanics are minimal: signing a document and transferring funds, which can happen in minutes via services like Clerky or Ironclad [1].

### Convertible Notes

Convertible debt is a loan that converts to equity at a future priced round. It carries a principal amount, interest rate (usually minimum 2%), and maturity date [1]. Key terms include the valuation cap (the maximum effective valuation the note holder pays, regardless of the round valuation) and a discount (a percentage off the round valuation) [1]. Investors view these terms as their "seed premium" [1].

### Equity Rounds

Equity rounds involve setting a per-share price and issuing new shares. They are more complicated, expensive, and time-consuming than SAFEs or notes, which is why they are now the exception for seed rounds [1]. Ralston provides a worked example: raising $1 million on a $5 million pre-money valuation with 10 million shares outstanding results in selling 2 million new shares at $0.50 each, yielding 16.7% dilution (not 20%, because dilution is calculated on post-money shares) [1].

Whatever financing form is used, Ralston advises using well-known documents like YC's SAFE: "These documents are well understood by the investor community, and have been drafted to be fair, yet founder friendly" [1].

## Types of Investors

The difference between angels and VCs, as Ralston characterizes it, is that "angels are amateurs and VCs are pros" [1]. Angels invest their own money and can make decisions quickly with a larger emotional component. VCs invest other people's money, require more meetings, and involve multiple partners in the decision [1].

The seed ecosystem also includes "super-angels" or "micro-VCs" that explicitly target very early stage companies, as well as platforms like AngelList Syndicates (which let angels pool resources behind a lead angel) and FundersClub (which invests selectively like a traditional VC) [1].

The best way to meet investors is through warm introductions. Demo days provide a concentrated opportunity to meet motivated seed investors [1].

## Building the Seed Pitch Deck

[[Aaron Harris]] provides YC's canonical seed deck template, emphasizing that "founders should strive for clarity and concision" [2]. The core insight is that most seed-stage companies do not have meaningful detail to explore, and when founders pretend otherwise, "their stories get muddled, and the investors get lost" [2].

Harris distills the philosophy to a single line: "Focus on narrative. The rest is commentary" [2].

### The YC Seed Deck Template

The template consists of these slides, each of which can expand to a set of up to 3 slides if needed (except the title, which must be exactly 1 slide) [2]:

1. **Title** - Company name and one-line description of what you do [2]
2. **Problem** - Clearly state the problem with specifics on how it impacts real people or businesses [2]
3. **Solution** - Explain what you do in as few words as possible, describing concrete benefits [2]
4. **Traction** - Show metrics with clear, meaningful numbers. Revenue is better than other metrics [2]
5. **Additional Metrics** - If available [2]
6. **Insights / Unique Advantage** - What makes this work, what your insights are [2]
7. **Business Model** - How you make money. "You probably don't know all the details yet, but you should know a lot of them" [2]
8. **Market** - Market size and growth. "Convince the investor that they're going to make lots of money with you" [2]
9. **Team** - What makes the founding team well suited to the problem. "This should be about founders. Nobody cares about your advisors" [2]
10. **The Ask** - How much money you need and what it buys. "If you can lay out where you'll be inside of a year, which should make you Series A ready, that's powerful" [2]

![YC Seed Deck Title Slide Template](images/2u-how-to-build-your-seed-round-pitch-deck/08553ccc0cf9.jpeg)

Harris notes that the deck is "intentionally simple in design" and references [[Kevin Hale]]'s piece on [how to design decks well](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck) for visual design guidance [2]. [[Jared Friedman]] suggested the creation of this template [2].

## Meeting and Closing Investors

### Meeting Strategy

The goal of an initial meeting is not to close but to get the next meeting [1]. Investors rarely commit on the first day. Ralston's rules for meetings:

- Research the investor's preferences and investment history [1]
- Simplify the pitch to essentials: great product (with demo), right team, huge opportunity [1]
- Listen more than you talk. "If you can get the investor to talk more than you, your probability of a deal skyrockets" [1]
- Strike a balance between confidence and humility. "Never cross over into arrogance, avoid defensiveness, but also don't be a pushover" [1]
- Never leave without an attempted close or absolute clarity on next steps [1]

### Closing Strategy

The hardest part is getting the first money in [1]. Once one investor commits, each subsequent close gets faster and easier. Ralston advises a "greedy" approach: "breadth-first search weighted by expected value" [1]. This means talking to as many people as possible while prioritizing those most likely to close.

When an investor says yes, close immediately using a handshake protocol. "If you fail at negotiating from this point on, it is probably your fault" [1].

### Negotiation Principles

- Do not negotiate in real-time. Take requests away and consult advisors or legal counsel [1]
- Most terms from credible investors tend to be reasonable [1]
- Ask investors to explain precisely what they are asking for and why [1]
- "Get the best deal you can get--but get the deal!" The valuation at this early round will seldom determine the company's success or failure [1]

### Documents Required

Ralston cautions against over-preparing documents for seed rounds: "If an investor is asking for too much due diligence or financials, they are almost certainly someone to avoid" [1]. The required documents are:

- **Executive Summary**: One page (two at most) covering vision, product, team, traction, market size, and minimum financials [1]
- **Slide Deck**: A coherent leave-behind with graphics, charts, and screenshots rather than walls of text [1]

### What Not to Do

Ralston lists specific behaviors to avoid when communicating with investors, including: being dishonest, being arrogant, talking so much investors cannot speak, being slow to follow up, creating detailed financials at seed stage, using unjustified market size numbers, asking for NDAs, trying to play investors against each other, and taking a "no" personally [1].

As [[Paul Graham]] advises: "If the soda is empty, stop making that awful sucking sound with the straw" [1].

## Key Rules for Seed Fundraising

Ralston consolidates his advice into a set of rules [1]:

- Get fundraising over as soon as possible and return to building
- But do not stop raising too early if fundraising is difficult
- Once someone says yes, get documents signed and money in the bank immediately
- Always hustle for leads
- "Never screw anyone over. Hold yourself and others on your team to the highest ethical standards. The Valley is a very small place, and a bad reputation is difficult to repair"
- Stay organized; co-founders should split tasks
- Have thick skin but balance confidence with humility

## References

1. [A Guide to Seed Fundraising](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4A-a-guide-to-seed-fundraising) — Geoff Ralston
