2. [How to Build Your Seed Round Pitch Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2u-how-to-build-your-seed-round-pitch-deck) — [[Aaron Harris]]

title: Enterprise Sales
body:
# Enterprise Sales

Ryan Junee argues that enterprise sales requires the same skill set as hacking -- the founder just needs to reframe the problem. Where a computer system is a network of hardware and software, a large enterprise is a network of people and processes. The emergent behavior of a corporation results from incentives and structured rules among its constituent people, much like the output of a computer system results from structured rules and data flows [1].

Enterprise sales, then, involves understanding and influencing the components (people) of the system to produce the desired behavior (buying your product). Every system (company) is different, requiring a systematic approach [1].

This article focuses on early-stage companies selling to Fortune 500 companies in high-touch, complex sales with $1M+ deals -- often called "elephant hunting" [1]. It assumes the company has found [[Product-Market Fit]] or is close to it.

## The People in the System

Junee maps out the typical roles a founder will encounter inside a target enterprise:

### Champion

A champion is an ally within the target company who has deep familiarity with the pain the product solves. "When you give your pitch, you will find the champion nodding along and completing your sentences for you" [1]. Champions will do everything they can to get the product deployed. Their influence depends on seniority -- line-level champions are useful, but director-level and above are necessary. Champions may become so excited they want to quit and join the startup (Junee reports this happening multiple times). "These people are gold. Find them early and build strong relationships quickly" [1].

### Detractor

The opposite of a champion. Detractors oppose deployment for various reasons: it threatens their job, they are invested in a competing product, or they built the current solution. The strategy is to stay off their radar and rally enough support from champions and other parts of the organization to overpower them [1].

### IT

Two types exist: the risk-avoidant organization that perceives change as threatening, and the forward-thinking one that embraces change. The former is far more common at large enterprises. The general strategy is to avoid IT until required for security reviews and approvals. By that time, business units should be "pulling" so strongly that IT cannot slow things down [1].

Junee warns against building features that IT requests without validating with actual users: "I've been burned by this in the past, building features that IT have asked for in order to get a deal done only to discover when putting the product in front of actual users that it wasn't what they wanted" [1].

### Procurement

After a decision maker agrees to buy, procurement negotiates the deal. They are typically incentivized to get the lowest price. The strategy: "make sure you are prepared to 'give' them something -- once they extract their pound of flesh they will get out of the way" [1]. This dynamic is why much enterprise software has a high list price with 80% or more discounting as standard practice [1].

### Legal

Legal's weapon of choice is "exhaustion -- sending back endless redlines over clauses in the contract that in reality won't have much impact" [1]. The strategy: decide upfront what matters and what you will concede, then after a round or two of redlines, have a frank discussion listing your non-negotiables and giving in on the rest. Combined with pressure from your champion telling legal to hurry, this closes the negotiation [1].

### Finance

Finance thinks about budget cycles. They may prefer to pay more upfront to use a quarter's budget rather than spacing payments over time. Whether they prefer capex or opex affects pricing model and sales team structure. Understanding their preference for lump sum purchase vs. monthly license fees is important [1].

## Tactics for Hacking the Enterprise

### Map and Understand the System

Spend significant time listening and learning before attempting to influence. Map out the people in the target company, draw a diagram. "Just like a great hacker spends a lot of time poking around, exploring and understanding how a system works before attempting to influence it, you need to spend a lot of time really understanding the organization, motivations and interactions" [1].

### Build Champion Relationships

Large enterprise deals will fall through before they close, maybe more than once. Champions revive the deal and keep it moving. "You should be able to text them any time of night and ask for their help" [1].

### Hire the Right Salespeople

In early-stage companies, Junee optimizes for skills and raw characteristics over experience. The most common mistake is hiring the wrong fit for the stage -- a "coin-operated" salesperson from a large company when you need someone comfortable with uncertainty who can learn quickly and has the intelligence to understand the system well enough to influence it. The large-company salesperson is, to use the analogy, a [script kiddie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Script_kiddie) rather than a hacker [1].

### Engage on Multiple Fronts

Create pairs of relationships: executives with executives, engineers with IT and security, product people with business unit leaders. One person (usually the account executive) should oversee and coordinate all interactions [1].

### Instrument the Process

Measure the sales process like using a packet sniffer or debugger. Collecting data and generating reports is essential to improving the process over time [1].

### Remember That Companies Are People

"Decisions are made by people, and people don't always act rationally." Friendships lead to partnerships, and friendships transcend the employer. Nurturing relationships pays off across multiple companies [1].

## References

1. [Enterprise Sales for Hackers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4m-enterprise-sales-for-hackers) -- Ryan Junee

title: Becoming a Founder
body:
# Becoming a Founder

[[Michael Seibel]] addresses the question of how to prepare for founding a startup from two angles: advice for college students on decisions that will help or hinder their ability to start a company later, and a framework for understanding the three career paths in tech [1] [2].

## Decisions That Block Future Founders

Seibel frequently talks to people in their late 20s and 30s who want to start startups but cannot. A decision they made when they were younger is either blocking or creating a serious barrier. The most common blockers [1]:

- They did not learn how to write code
- They never met anyone in college who knew how to code
- They significantly increased their expenses early in life, did not save, and now cannot afford to begin a company
- They created roots post-college in a city that is not startup-friendly

## Advice for College Students

Seibel frames this as what those blocked founders would tell their younger selves [1]:

**Learn to code.** This does not mean majoring in Computer Science. College offers a chance to learn the basics. Being a programmer gives the ability to work and start a company in any industry. "You don't have to be the best developer, and you don't have to write code your entire life if you don't want to -- but knowing how to code gives you the proverbial golden ticket." The number one barrier to starting a tech startup is not knowing how to code [1].

**Meet people with real skills.** College puts you next to designers, programmers, salespeople, and scientists. Make friends with people who have different skills. These people could become cofounders or early employees [1].

**Have fun.** For most successful founders, college grades and accolades had almost nothing to do with their future success. "Personally, the greatest career opportunity college gave me was the chance to make friendships with really smart people" [1].

**Take responsibility.** Join the leadership of a club where you must execute on a real-world task -- organizing a conference, creating a publication, volunteer work, playing on a sports team. Learn what you are good and bad at when working with others [1].

**Be ready to hit the ground running.** Seibel disagrees with the cultural norm of using your 20s to find yourself. "Your 20s are your most valuable working years: You often have very few obligations (no family), a very low burn rate (you're used to living in a dorm), and you are still young enough to meet new people and learn new skills." Use college to get partying out of your system and spend your 20s working hard [1].

**Be your own career counselor.** Career counseling at most universities is "really really bad." Use some college time to figure out what you want to do after graduation. Having a plan, even one you change, is better than no plan [1].

**Ignore parental pressure if needed.** Many people pursue careers because of parental pressure and poor career counseling. "The number of doctors and lawyers I know who really don't love their jobs or their lives is astounding" [1].

**Be careful with student debt.** Student debt forces people away from startups and into big companies. Once in a big company, expenses spin up (car, nice apartment) and the person prices themselves out of starting a company. Expensive private schools without good financial aid are worth avoiding. The best private schools are generous with financial aid [1].

Seibel states that the prime startup years are ages 22 to 32. "Every year you are not doing a startup you are losing 10% of this prime time" [1].

## The Three Paths in Tech

Seibel outlines three primary career paths in the tech industry: founder, executive, and employee. He leaves out investor because the best path to becoming an investor starts with being successful (or failing) at one of these three [2].

### Founder

**Pros**: Work on something you are passionate about; bring something new into the world; high responsibility inspires extreme productivity; choose who you work with; learn new skills at an extremely fast pace [2].

**Cons**: Incredibly stressful (even success hurts); probably will not maximize personal earnings; significant financial, skillset, and location hurdles to get started; large-scale success often requires a decade-plus commitment; commitment level can significantly hurt personal relationships [2].

**Strategy**: The initial goal is to accumulate four prerequisites [2]:
1. Potential teammates with the technical skills to build the MVP
2. A financial plan (savings, friends/family investment, bootstrapping, or reduced spending to create a 6-12 month runway)
3. A problem that you and your potential teammates are passionate about solving
4. An idea for how to solve the problem (the least important prerequisite, but still required)

A popular mistake is trying to hustle around a missing prerequisite instead of solving the underlying issue. If the team lacks technical skills, do not work with a dev shop -- make friends with people who have those skills and convince them to join [2].

When hard barriers prevent starting immediately, Seibel recommends moving to a tech hub (preferably the Bay Area) and working for a tech company until you can save money, meet the right teammates, or discover the right problem. "It usually takes at least 10 years to build a large and impactful company. It's fine to delay your start date in order to give yourself the best chance of success" [2].

**Experience is overvalued.** "In almost all cases, no matter what knowledge you bring to the table, you will learn most of what you need to know about your problem, your customer, and the best solution after you start your company" [2].

### Executive (Senior Manager at a Large Company)

**Pros**: Stable income and benefits; high prestige (only very successful founders have more); a higher likelihood of having a huge impact (given most startups fail); does not require building a team and acquiring money [2].

**Cons**: Producing results is not necessarily how you move up -- internal politics are as important or more; success can be blocked by others inside the organization; requires picking companies that will be growing and successful for a long time; takes a long time to get significant responsibility [2].

**Two strategies** [2]:
1. **Pick a fast-growing company early.** If the company grows rapidly for years, opportunities to become an executive emerge. This is similar to a VC's task of picking winners. Example: being one of the first 100 people at Facebook and staying ten years.
2. **Move diagonally up between established companies.** Start at Google, get hired at Dropbox as a team lead, move to Yahoo to become a director, move back to Google. This requires constantly watching for better opportunities inside and outside the current company.

### Employee (Individual Contributor / Middle Manager)

**Pros**: Stable income and benefits; more work and fewer meetings; more often directly affecting the customer; flexibility in where and how much you work with a high-demand skill set; more time for friends and family [2].

**Cons**: Productivity can be blocked by bad management; often no control over what you work on; no voice in major decisions; harder to become very wealthy; can be boring; easier to be fired if skills or productivity decline [2].

**Strategy**: Similar to the executive path. Either spot and join a quickly growing company, or find a way into a well-known successful company. It is much easier to move between brand-name companies when you start at a brand-name company. The path is easier to optimize as a software developer [2].

### The Cost of Exploration

Seibel warns against overextending the discovery phase. If it takes 5-10 years to get truly good at something and 10 years are spent discovering what to get good at, "it's going to take a long time for you to feel like a highly skilled productive person." Exploration is not wrong, but its costs must be understood and planned for [2].

## References

1. [A Letter to College Students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4d-a-letter-to-college-students) — [[Michael Seibel]] (March 2017)
2. [Three Paths in the Tech Industry: Founder, Executive, or Employee](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4h-three-paths-in-the-tech-industry-founder-executive-or-employee) — [[Michael Seibel]] (September 2017)

title: Network Effects
body:
# Network Effects

Network effects occur when a product becomes more valuable as more people use it. The concept is central to understanding why certain startups achieve winner-take-most dynamics. [[Anu Hariharan]]'s case studies of Chinese technology companies provide some of the most detailed analyses of network effects in the YC canon.

## Types of Network Effects

Hariharan draws a distinction between Metcalfe's law and Reed's law in her analysis of [[Pinduoduo]]:

**Metcalfe's law** holds that the value of a network grows proportionally to the square of the number of users. This applies to one-to-one connection networks like telephones or messaging platforms [1].

**Reed's law** holds that the value of a group-forming network grows exponentially with the ease of group formation. Hariharan identifies Pinduoduo as having "one of the most powerful network effects in the world" because it taps into groups of social relationships rather than one-to-one connections [1]. The team purchase mechanic means every user has an incentive to recruit others, and the recruiting happens through existing social groups.

## Network Effects in Practice

### WeChat

[[WeChat]]'s network effects compounded across layers. The messaging platform created a base network effect, then the Moments social feed added a content layer, then WeChat Pay added a financial layer, then Mini Programs added an application layer. Each layer reinforced the others, making switching costs enormous [2]. WeChat grew from 0 to 889 million MAUs by stacking network effects on top of each other.

### Toutiao

[[Toutiao]] demonstrates data network effects: more users generate more engagement data, which improves the recommendation algorithm, which makes the content more addictive, which attracts more users [3]. Algorithms without data are useless, and data without an addictive product is unavailable. The virtuous cycle compounds product quality over time.

### Pinduoduo

Pinduoduo's team purchase model creates network effects at the group level. Users recruit friends and family into purchasing groups to unlock lower prices, which brings new users to the platform, who then form their own groups [1]. The social graph becomes the distribution channel.

## Network Effects and Growth

[[Paul Graham]] notes that raising money lets founders "choose their growth rate," and the most successful startups take VC money even when profitable because it lets them grow faster and fend off competitors, "especially in businesses with network effects" [4]. Network effects create a strategic imperative for fast growth: the first company to reach scale often locks in its advantage.

## References

1. [Pinduoduo and the Rise of Social E-Commerce](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce) -- [[Anu Hariharan]], Nic Dardenne
2. [How WeChat Grew to Be the #1 App in the World](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world) -- [[Anu Hariharan]]
3. [The Hidden Forces Behind China's Content King Toutiao](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao) -- [[Anu Hariharan]]
4. [Startup = Growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth) -- [[Paul Graham]] (September 2012)

title: Startup Outcomes and Optionality
body:
# Startup Outcomes and Optionality

[[Sam Altman]] addresses a common but rarely discussed situation: the startup that is successful but is not on a trajectory to be a billion-dollar company. Using a metaphor attributed to David Weiden, he describes starting a startup as being pushed off a cliff with a bag of aerospace parts [1].

## The Airplane vs. Spaceship Framework

Sometimes the parts make a spaceship -- huge market, great product, natural monopoly, credible path to being the winner. Plenty of advice exists for that case. Sometimes the parts make nothing at all. But "a reasonable amount of the time, it turns out you have the parts for an airplane" [1].

An airplane is a company with profitability within reach, on track to be worth $100 million with several more years of hard work. "This is nothing to be ashamed of -- quite the opposite, obviously. This is a triumph by almost any metric." It is what happens to "most very lucky, very smart, very determined people who decide to start a startup and have everything go their way except happening upon a perfect market" [1].

The founder has two choices: try to get a new bag of parts (new market, new product, very different strategy) and keep trying to build a spaceship, or decide to have a really good airplane [1].

## Founder-Investor Misalignment

The most important thing to understand: this is where founders and investors often get misaligned. Investors are looking for spaceships; airplanes do not matter much to them, though they matter quite a bit to founders [1].

Existing investors will usually be willing to take almost any amount of risk to turn an airplane into a spaceship. The typical advice offered is little more than "point your plane at the moon, burn more fuel, and let's see what happens" [1].

Altman's recommendation: "Be frank and realistic with your existing investors; you will eventually persuade them that a small multiple is better than a loss." The best investors understand how venture investing works and do not fight over such outcomes -- many treat founders extra-generously hoping to fund their next company. The longer founders delude their investors, the worse shape they end up in [1].

## The Overvaluation Trap

Raising too much money at too high a valuation severely limits optionality. "Very often I've seen cases where founders know in their hearts they have an airplane but are able to convince good investors it might still be a spaceship. This really causes a lot of heartache, and often precludes your opportunity for a good acquisition later" [1].

The answer is simple in theory but difficult in practice: do not raise a lot of money at a high price when you think the company will probably not increase 10x in value and you would be happy selling soon. This may require changing how the company is run and finding a path to profitability with current capital. "It's far less painful to deal with this now rather than putting the company in a position that will preclude the option you think is best" [1].

## Positioning for Acquisition

Companies get bought, not sold. It is very difficult to force an acquisition, and nearly impossible if the company has not built something legitimately valuable. "Somehow, this fact surprises founders who decide 'we'd like to get acquired' almost every time" [1].

What founders can do: begin developing relationships with potential acquirers early. It usually takes a long time, and personal relationships help [1].

The outcome is still worth pride. "Silicon Valley is filled with people who had one or several mid-success companies before eventually hitting it out of the park" [1].

## References

1. [Navigating Success for Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4s-navigating-success-for-your-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]]

title: Runway Management
body:
# Runway Management

Running low on runway is one of the most dangerous situations a startup can face, not only because of the financial reality but because of its psychological effects on founders. Dalton Caldwell addresses this directly: "The hardest part of dealing with a low runway situation is managing your own psychology. You have to simultaneously manage your own anxiety to not be overly negative about your prospects, but also not be irrationally positive" [1].

This article covers how to assess runway, the psychology of low-runway situations, counterproductive thinking patterns, practical cost reduction, and the decision to shut down.

## Prerequisites: Know Where You Stand

Before anything else, founders must understand exactly how much cash and runway they have. Caldwell directs founders to two essential readings by [[Paul Graham]]: [The Fatal Pinch](http://paulgraham.com/pinch.html) and [Default Alive or Default Dead](http://paulgraham.com/aord.html) [1].

The core framework: if you are Default Dead (your current trajectory leads to running out of money before reaching profitability), it is your responsibility as a founder to immediately take actions to become Default Alive [1]. The mechanisms are straightforward: "Either you need to grow revenue more quickly, cut costs, or both" [1].

## Counterproductive Ideas

Caldwell identifies seven common thought patterns that cause founders to rationalize inaction when they are Default Dead [1]:

1. **"The Fatal Pinch does not apply to me"** - Believing your company is a special exception
2. **"Investors will continue to fund my company if I run out of money"** - Assuming future fundraising is guaranteed
3. **"If I fail at fundraising I can just sell the company"** - Treating acquisition as a reliable fallback
4. **"My conversations with potential acquirers or investors are very far along and likely to happen"** - Confusing activity with commitment
5. **"Acquirers won't buy us if I cut costs"** - Believing a smaller team reduces acquisition attractiveness
6. **"My employee morale will plummet if I cut costs"** - Prioritizing comfort over survival
7. **"New investors won't fund us if I cut costs"** - Believing cost-cutting signals weakness to investors

Caldwell warns: "Don't let these ideas be the justification(s) for why you choose to remain Default Dead" [1].

## Understanding Negotiating Leverage

Caldwell presents a graph showing how leverage deteriorates over time for a company with declining runway [1]:

![Runway and Leverage Graph](images/3Z-advice-for-companies-with-less-than-1-year-of-runway/a8feb33288f6.png)

Three lessons from this graph [1]:

1. **Delaying action is a bad strategy.** Make changes to become Default Alive now, not later.
2. **Game theory works against you.** From a game theory perspective, an investor or acquirer benefits by stalling and dragging you along until you have no leverage. "An opportunistic acquirer or investor is unlikely to say 'no' outright, and will keep their options open as you become increasingly desperate."
3. **Without corrective action, things get worse.** "If things look bleak now and you take no corrective action, it is overwhelmingly likely the situation will get worse, not better."

## Reducing Burn

The least painful option is to dramatically increase revenue immediately [1]. When that is not possible, cost cuts must follow a clear priority.

### What to Cut First

1. **PR, marketing expenses, perks, and parties** - The easiest to cut. "Don't blow your money on this stuff" [1]
2. **Payroll** - The most likely source of high burn. As noted in [[Paul Graham]]'s Fatal Pinch, "over-hiring is usually the root cause of high burn" [1]
3. **Real estate/lease costs** - Binding agreements and very difficult to exit. "Real estate obligations are a common cause of death for later stage companies" [1]

### Handling Layoffs

If staff reductions are necessary [1]:
- Treat former employees well
- Be transparent with remaining employees
- "You should always treat your staff as you would want to be treated"

## The Point of No Return

When a company has less than three months of cash, founders must face the possibility of shutdown head-on [1]. Caldwell identifies less than two months as "the point of no return" in many cases [1].

At this stage, the required actions are [1]:
- Immediately lay off employees and provide severance
- Pay down obligations
- Use remaining cash for shutdown costs

The consequences of not acting: "If you don't do this and instead end up with zero cash and outstanding payroll, tax or other obligations, things will get Very Bad" [1].

### Shutdown Principles

Caldwell lists specific considerations for an orderly shutdown [1]:

- **Shut down when you hit the point of no return.** Do not drag things out.
- **Do not become insolvent.** Pay debts, especially tax and payroll obligations.
- **Consult lawyers.** In messy scenarios, founders can face personal liability.
- **Avoid no-upside situations.** "Don't drag things out and end up in a no upside situation: no upside for you, for your employees, for your investors, or for your customers."
- **Behave with integrity.** "Even if things go poorly, behave in a way you would be proud of."

### On Reputation and Defeat

Caldwell acknowledges the psychological difficulty: "Often the toughest thing for a founder that has made it this far is to 'admit defeat'" [1]. His advice on reputation: "it's just as important to handle situations well when things go poorly as it is when they are going great" [1].

## Two Things to Remember

Caldwell closes with two principles that override everything else [1]:

1. **Don't lie to yourself**
2. **Act quickly and decisively**

## References

1. [Advice for Companies with Less Than 1 Year of Runway](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3Z-advice-for-companies-with-less-than-1-year-of-runway) — Dalton Caldwell

title: Startup Leadership
body:
# Startup Leadership

Leadership in startups differs from leadership in established organizations because the role of the CEO changes dramatically as the company scales. [[Ali Rowghani]], drawing on his experience working closely with Ed Catmull, Steve Jobs, John Lasseter, and Bob Iger at Pixar, argues that leadership is measurable through three foundational traits and that a startup CEO's job shifts from building product to building a company. These two essays together form a comprehensive framework for understanding, measuring, and improving leadership in the startup context.

## Leadership Is Built on Trust

The central claim in Rowghani's framework is that all great leadership reduces to trust. Despite observing leaders with radically different styles (an introverted scientist, an extroverted artist, a college dropout infamous for brash behavior, and a career executive who was "exceptionally genteel and diplomatic"), Rowghani found they all shared the ability to "create an extraordinary amount of trust in the people around them" [1].

Trust is both a science and an art [1]:
- **The science**: Trust accrues when a leader's predictions about products, investments, and competitive landscapes prove accurate, and when their people decisions prove correct. Over time, the track record becomes known.
- **The art**: Trust is built when leaders "say the right thing at the right time, and show empathy and good judgment." It grows when leaders "stand for ideals bigger than yourself rather than caring primarily about your personal success, wealth, fame, or position."

This is why leadership styles cannot be copied: "You can't try to copy Steve Jobs or Ed Catmull in your quest to be a great leader. You can only be yourself" [1].

## The Three Foundational Traits

See [[Three Traits of Great Leadership]] for the full framework. In summary:

### 1. Clarity of Thought and Communication

Great leaders think clearly and describe a compelling vision [1]. Rowghani cites Jeff Bezos's retail strategy as an exemplary case: it was built on three enduring customer preferences (lower prices, bigger selection, faster delivery). As Bezos said: "You can build a business strategy around the things that are stable in time....when you have something that you know is true, even over the long-term, you can afford to put a lot of energy into it" [1].

Clarity of thought always precedes clarity of language. The practical advice: "spend more time thinking about what you believe is truly important for your business. Once you've crystallized what's important for everyone to understand, practice expressing it in simple terms" [1].

As companies scale, preparation becomes critical. Tobi Lütke of Shopify and his senior team spend hundreds of hours preparing for their annual employee Summit. As Lütke describes it: "We want to be a loosely coupled, highly aligned company. The Summit is the main enabler of this because it is a grand sync. We spend countless hours preparing because if we communicate well at the Summit, we achieve great alignment by the end" [1].

### 2. Judgment About People

Great leaders have strong intuition about who to give power and responsibility to. They "are able to see hidden potential in people and detect cases where ambition exceeds ability" [1]. When hiring or promotion mistakes happen, they have the courage to act.

The stakes are high: "Nothing does more damage to an organization or to the standing of a leader than picking the wrong leaders or failing to correct these mistakes when they happen" [1]. However, "leaders who fire too many of their own also lose a lot of credibility and trust" [1].

To improve people judgment, Rowghani recommends gathering more data: meet the best people in every field to sharpen pattern recognition. He cites the example of Uber CTO Thuan Pham being interviewed by Travis Kalanick for "30 hours straight, one-on-one, over two weeks" including over Skype while traveling. Pham recalled: "Throughout those 30 hours, I actually forgot it was an interview. It was just like a discussion between two colleagues" [1].

Reference checks are essential, particularly for behavioral evidence of good judgment and integrity, which are "hard to test in an interview" [1].

### 3. Personal Integrity and Commitment

Integrity means "standing for something meaningful beyond oneself rather than being motivated by narrow personal interests" [1]. It includes admitting mistakes, receiving critical feedback with humility, and avoiding behaviors that erode trust (favoritism, conflicts of interest, inappropriate relationships).

Rowghani proposes a demanding test: "if your team had full transparency into your private communications and behavior towards employees, would you be embarrassed by anything you have done or said? This is a high bar, but one that great leaders strive to meet" [1].

Commitment goes beyond working long hours. Great leaders "make their work into their core life mission in ways that inspire others" [1].

## Measuring Leadership

Rowghani argues that measuring leadership through bottom-line company performance fails because "timing and luck can be confounding factors" and because performance metrics offer "no clues as to how someone can improve as a leader" [1].

Instead, he proposes systematic employee surveys organized around the three traits. The full survey framework appears in [[Three Traits of Great Leadership]]. Key elements include asking both the CEO and employees parallel questions about clarity of mission, quality of people decisions, and integrity of leadership behavior [1].

He recommends startups begin gathering this data systematically at approximately 50 people [1].

## The CEO's Evolving Role

The CEO's job changes dramatically across three phases of company growth. See [[Startup Leadership]] for the complete framework.

### Phase 1: Doer-in-Chief

The CEO's first job is to "build a great product and find a small group of people who love it and use it enthusiastically" [2]. In this phase, the CEO must be deeply involved in building the product, acquiring users, and conserving money. "Delegation should not be a word in your vocabulary" [2].

### Phase 2: Company-Builder-in-Chief (20-500 employees)

When demand exceeds the small team's capacity, the CEO must shift to company-building [2]. This is the critical transition that determines the startup's long-term success. In practice, Phase 2 begins around 20-25 employees and ends around 400-500 [2].

The transition is psychologically difficult: "When you've been a successful Doer-in-Chief, it's hard to stop. It's hard to stop coding, designing product specs, and interacting with customers on a daily basis" [2]. It can also create confusion with the team, who may wonder why the CEO is suddenly delegating tasks they had always done [2].

The key insight: "once your startup reaches 20-30 people, you'll have to spend more time leading (i.e., directing the activities of others). And since time is finite, the only way to lead more is do less" [2].

David Rusenko, co-founder of Weebly, illustrates successful delegation: "Often, the first time I find out about a product feature is reading about it on our blog. It shocks most founders to hear this, but I know I've done my job well because I've yet to see a feature that was built poorly. You should aspire to build a team that's so good that you don't have to be involved in the product details" [2].

### Phase 3: Investment in Transformation

At the end of Phase 2, the leadership team operates at a high level with minimal CEO involvement. The CEO can then shift to taking profits from the core business and investing in transformative new products [2]. Facebook exemplifies this: it built its senior management team in Phase 2 while operating at roughly breakeven, then used Phase 3 profits to fund Messenger as a separate product and acquire Instagram, WhatsApp, and Oculus [2].

## Three Non-Delegable CEO Tasks

Rowghani identifies three tasks that only the CEO can perform. See [[Startup Leadership]] for full detail.

### 1. Hiring and Managing the Leadership Team

"Only the CEO can hire the company's senior leadership team and make sure that they work well together" [2]. The hiring decisions must be the CEO's because "the consequences are yours as well" [2]. Patrick Collison made it a point to meet with the "best-in-the-world" in each field to calibrate what great looks like [2].

Key metrics for this task:
- Expect at least 25% of leadership hires not to work out [2]
- Most CEOs take too long to fire their first executive [2]
- "The longer you leave an under-performing executive in place, the more credibility you lose with everyone else on your team" [2]
- Do not be surprised if 50% of time goes to hiring and managing the senior team [2]

### 2. Creating Purpose and Alignment (Mission-to-Metrics)

See [[Mission-to-Metrics Framework]]. The CEO defines the Mission (purpose), Strategy (direction), and Metrics (pace and performance) [2]. Rowghani illustrates perfect alignment with the SpaceX employee who, when asked "What is your job?", traced his specific task (designing a steering system) back through the company's strategy (reusable rockets) to the mission (colonize Mars) [2].

Key principles:
- Write your strategy in long form to catch flaws in thinking [2]
- Do not equate metrics with top-line results like revenue growth. Instead, find the drivers. Facebook discovered that connecting a new user to 10 friends within 14 days correlated with retained usage, so they set that as their key product metric [2]
- Communicate Mission-to-Metrics constantly. "You have to reiterate the Mission-to-Metrics much more than what feels reasonable" [2]

### 3. Nurturing Company Culture

Unlike the other two tasks, culture is not solely the CEO's job; it is everyone's responsibility [2]. The CEO should not write cultural values alone. Instead, "encourage co-founders and early employees to work together to codify a set of values and behavioral norms that feel authentic and aspirational to everyone" [2].

Rowghani tells the story of Toy Story 2 at Pixar as the defining cultural moment. With seven months to release, creative leadership felt the film was not working. Disney argued it was too late to start over. Pixar refused, tearing up the story and rewriting from scratch, pushing the studio "to the brink of collapse" to complete the new version on time [2]. The film was a success, and afterward, the executive team closed the studio for two months to let everyone recuperate.

Ed Catmull called it "the most intense and important period in the studio's history: 'Toy Story 2 defined us. It said that we couldn't be a studio that produced both great work and average work. Everything we made had to be great'" [2].

## The Measure of Success

Rowghani cites Steve Jobs at a Pixar management meeting: "When I'm at my best, 50% of my time is unscheduled. That's the time I use to think, drop in on the people I want to speak with, and let my curiosity roam. It's my time to be creative. Without this free time, I would never be able to stay ahead of the company. To lead a company, you've always got to be two steps ahead. There's no way to lead a company from behind" [2].

Having significant unscheduled time is "perhaps the clearest sign of success for a Phase 2 CEO" because it signals successful delegation, a strong leadership team, and well-codified Mission-to-Metrics [2].

## References

1. [How to Measure Leadership](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3j-how-to-measure-leadership) — [[Ali Rowghani]]
2. [The Second Job of a Startup CEO](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3k-the-second-job-of-a-startup-ceo) — [[Ali Rowghani]]

title: Getting Press
body:
# Getting Press

[[Michael Seibel]] describes the press strategy he advises for YC companies, built on advice he received from Mike Arrington and M.G. Siegler at an SV Angel CEO conference [1]. The core principle is four words:

**"Treat PR like biz dev."**

Before hearing this advice, Seibel wasted mental energy on presumed special rules for press: catchy subject lines, blasting lists of a hundred reporters, writing press releases. Most of these efforts failed. Treating PR like business development means developing relationships with reporters as people, getting introduced through warm connections instead of cold emails, and creating a fair value exchange [1].

## Step One: Generate News

Founders commonly pitch reporters profile pieces and stories that could be written at any time. Reporters do not find these valuable. What makes a valuable story is news -- something timely, something that makes more sense to talk about today than tomorrow [1].

Four categories of startup news [1]:

**Product Launches and Features**: Things coming out today, not yesterday or tomorrow. User-facing features. At Socialcam, Seibel would reach out to the press for video filters, soundtrack capability, or user profiles.

**Product/Sales Milestones**: Hitting a million users, $100K MRR, a million guests per night (as with Airbnb). The number should be as big as possible relative to the startup's size. Seibel recommends sandbagging: wait until 25% past the milestone to announce, so the next milestone is that much closer.

**Significant BD Deals or Customers**: Landing a team at Airbnb or Instacart, or partnering with GM to build self-driving cars. If you have their permission, consider announcing it.

**Fundraising**: For better or worse, fundraising is conflated with traction and success. Angel rounds or Series A/B/etc. are considered news by most reporters and noticed by potential employees.

## Step Two: Make Your Own Press Contacts

Reporters want to talk to CEOs and cofounders, not PR people. As an early-stage founder, building reporter relationships and making pitches directly produces a higher success rate and costs less time and money. Seibel's team spent over $100,000 on PR agencies and PR people as an early-stage startup before learning this [1].

The best way to meet a tech reporter is through a warm intro from the CEO or a cofounder. For YC founders, the YC network is helpful because the target reporter has almost certainly written about someone in the network. Proper etiquette is to ask your friend for an intro to only one reporter at a time [1].

The intro message should be readable and reply-able in thirty seconds [1]:
- One sentence describing the news
- For an exclusive: say you are offering an exclusive
- For an embargo: include the date and time of the announcement
- A request for a twenty-minute call

On the call, have three to five reasons ready for why the news and the company are important. At the end, offer notes and relevant collateral (screenshots, graphs, logos, pictures). This provides reporters with the language you prefer while being courteous [1].

## Step Three: Pitch an Exclusive

Almost all startups should offer their story as an exclusive to one tech reporter at a time. The target audience -- investors, potential employees, motivated customers, early adopters -- is concentrated in a few tech outlets. Getting blanket coverage across many tech blogs is often not worth the struggle and can create problems when embargoes are broken [1].

## Step Four: Consistent Cadence

The PR industry philosophy of maximizing press mentions per story (packing all news into one big announcement) is not optimal for startups. Seibel compares PR benefits to funding basic research in science: "you can't predict the advances but they do happen when you invest consistently over time" [1].

One story every month or every other month produces better results than one huge story every year. Spread news across multiple stories instead of combining everything. Maintain a queue of three to five pieces of news to announce in the coming six months. This removes pressure if one piece does not get picked up and helps keep a regular cadence [1].

## Expectations and Limits

A 25-50% success rate on pitches is reasonable when doing everything right. After six to twelve months of good cadence and real news, a founder should be relatively friendly with two to five reporters who will reply to most emails [1].

At a later stage, when press organically pays attention (Dropbox and Airbnb are examples), it becomes strategic to create distance between founders and the press. This is when PR people become helpful as a buffer for strategic announcements [1].

PR should occupy no more than 5% of a CEO's time. Founders should not expect PR to create observable miracles (tons of users, customers, or investment offers). Most benefits only become obvious weeks or months after a story is posted [1].

## References

1. [Getting Press for Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4c-getting-press-for-your-startup) — [[Michael Seibel]] (February 2017)

title: Financial Survival
body:
# Financial Survival

Nearly every startup that fails, fails by running out of money [5]. [[Paul Graham]] returns to this theme across multiple essays, developing increasingly specific frameworks for how founders should think about spending, fundraising, and the critical question of whether they will survive.

## The Default Alive / Default Dead Framework

Graham's October 2015 essay introduced the most important diagnostic question for startups past the first 8-9 months [1]: assuming expenses remain constant and revenue growth continues on its current trend, will the company make it to profitability on the money it has left? "Or to put it more dramatically, by default do they live or die?" [1]

The startling finding: half the founders Graham talks to do not know the answer [1]. Trevor Blackwell built a [calculator](http://growth.tlb.org/#) to help founders find out.

This question determines the entire conversation. If the company is default alive, you can talk about ambitious new things. If it is default dead, you need to talk about how to save it [1]. See [[Default Alive or Default Dead]] for the full framework.

## The Fatal Pinch

The most dangerous scenario is what Graham calls the "fatal pinch": default dead + slow growth + not enough time to fix it [1]. Founders end up in it by not realizing that is where they are headed.

A common death spiral [1]: A startup makes something moderately appealing and has decent initial growth. They raise their first round fairly easily because the founders seem smart and the idea sounds plausible. But growth is only ok, not great. The founders convince themselves that hiring a bunch of people will boost growth. Their investors agree. But the growth never comes because the product is only moderately appealing. Now they are rapidly running out of runway. They hope further investment will save them, but with high expenses and slow growth, they are unappealing to investors. They are unable to raise more, and the company dies.

The correct action: address the fundamental problem -- that the product is only moderately appealing. Hiring people is rarely the way to fix that. More often it makes it harder. "At this early stage, the product needs to evolve more than to be 'built out,' and that's usually easier with fewer people" [1].

## Overhiring: The Biggest Killer

"Don't hire too fast. Hiring too fast is by far the biggest killer of startups that raise money" [1].

Three reasons founders overhire [1]:
1. **There is so much work to do.** Naive founders think if they can hire enough people, it will all get done.
2. **Successful startups have lots of employees**, so it seems like that is what you do to be successful. But large staffs are probably more the effect of growth than the cause.
3. **They don't want to face the real reason for slow growth**: the product is not appealing enough.

VCs often push founders to overhire because kill-or-cure strategies are optimal for portfolios. "VCs want to blow you up, in one sense of the phrase or the other. But as a founder your incentives are different. You want above all to survive" [1].

Sam Altman reinforced this in a note on a draft of the essay: "I think it's roughly correct to say that YC's most successful companies have never been the fastest to hire, and one of the marks of a great founder is being able to resist this urge" [1].

Paul Buchheit added a related observation: "A related problem that I see a lot is premature scaling -- founders take a small business that isn't really working (bad unit economics, typically) and then scale it up because they want impressive growth numbers. This is similar to over-hiring in that it makes the business much harder to fix once it's big, plus they are bleeding cash really fast" [1].

Airbnb, by contrast, waited 4 months after raising money at the end of YC before hiring their first employee. The founders were overworked but were spending that time evolving Airbnb into the astonishingly successful organism it became [1].

## Plan A and Plan B

It is not safe to be default dead even if you think you can count on investors saving you [1]. Investors' interest is a function of growth. If you have steep revenue growth -- over 5x a year -- you can start to count on investor interest even if you are not profitable. But investors are fickle. Something about your business can spook them even if growth is great [1].

Graham's rule: "You should always have a plan B as well: you should know (as in write down) precisely what you'll need to do to survive if you can't raise more money, and precisely when you'll have to switch to plan B if plan A isn't working" [1].

The common mistake is to think of growing fast versus operating cheaply as a sharp dichotomy. "In practice there is surprisingly little connection between how much a startup spends and how fast it grows. When a startup grows fast, it's usually because the product hits a nerve. When a startup spends a lot, it's usually because the product is expensive to develop or sell, or simply because they're wasteful" [1].

## Raising Too Little, Too Much, or Just Right

Graham's 2006 essay identifies three fundraising-related mistakes that kill startups [2]:

**Raising too little (#11):** Startup funding is measured in time -- runway. You must take enough to get to the next step, whatever that is. Usually: if all you have is an idea, get to a working prototype. If you have a prototype, get to launch. If launched, get to significant growth [2]. Graham advises: "set both low, initially: spend practically nothing, and make your initial goal simply to build a solid prototype. This gives you maximum flexibility" [2].

**Spending too much (#12):** The classic way to burn cash is hiring a lot of people, which bites you twice: it increases costs and slows you down, because money that is being consumed faster has to last longer [2]. Three rules for hiring: (a) don't do it if you can avoid it, (b) pay people with equity rather than salary to attract the right kind of commitment, and (c) only hire people who will write code or get users -- those are the only things you need at first [2].

**Raising too much (#13):** Taking a lot of money changes the company's atmosphere and makes it harder to change direction. "When you raise a lot of money, your company moves to the suburbs and has kids" [2]. Once you hire a sales force to sell to companies, what happens when you realize you should be selling to consumers? "In practice, you don't realize that. The more people you have, the more you stay pointed in the same direction" [2].

The time cost is also significant. Raising VC-scale money is "more work, probably, than the startup itself" [2]. Graham's advice: "take the first reasonable deal you get. If you get an offer from a reputable firm at a reasonable valuation with no unusually onerous terms, just take it and get on with building the company. Economically, startups are an all-or-nothing game. Bargain-hunting among investors is a waste of time" [2].

## The Equity Equation

When trading stock for anything -- money, employees, deals -- Graham provides a formula for whether to do it: you should give up n% of your company if what you trade it for improves your average outcome enough that the (100 - n)% you have left is worth more than the whole company was before [3]. The formula is 1/(1 - n). See [[Equity Equation]] for the full treatment.

## Poverty as Advantage

Graham identifies poverty as a genuine advantage for young founders [5]. Nearly every startup that fails, fails by running out of money, and a low burn rate gives more opportunity to recover from the many mistakes startups make at first [5].

Recent graduates can live on practically nothing, which gives them an edge. "The guys with kids and mortgages are at a real disadvantage" [5]. When Robert Morris and Graham started Viaweb at ages 29 and 30, they still lived like 23-year-olds with roughly zero assets. "In retrospect having nothing turned out to be convenient" [5].

Even more important than living cheaply is thinking cheaply. The Apple II was popular partly because it was cheap -- Woz designed it for himself and couldn't afford anything more. Viaweb's top-level service was $300/month, an order of magnitude below the norm, because $300 seemed like a lot to them. "Like Apple, we created something inexpensive, and therefore popular, simply because we were poor" [5].

## Investor Management

Founders must manage investors without being controlled by them [2]. Ignoring investors is probably less dangerous than caving in to them. "If the founders know what they're doing, it's better to have half their attention focused on the product than the full attention of investors who don't" [2].

The degree of control investors exert usually depends on how much money you have taken. With VC-scale funding, investors may have board majority and are literally your bosses [2]. Even Google and Apple had significant investor trouble early on. Apple's board made a "nearly fatal blunder" in firing Steve Jobs [2].

## Don't Sacrifice Users to Supposed Profit

Making money is not what kills startups. Not making something users want is what kills them [2]. "Because making something people want is so much harder than making money from it, you should leave business models for later, just as you'd leave some trivial but messy feature for version 2" [2].

Google made search work, then worried about how to make money from it. It is irresponsible not to think about business models, but "it's ten times more irresponsible not to think about the product" [2].

## References

1. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2015)
2. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
3. [The Equity Equation](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8u-the-equity-equation) -- [[Paul Graham]] (July 2007)
4. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2006)
5. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)

title: Series A Term Sheets
body:
# Series A Term Sheets

Jason Kwon and [[Aaron Harris]] of YC describe a common problem: founders seeing their first term sheet do not know what "good" looks like [1]. This puts them at a disadvantage because VCs see term sheets constantly. YC, having seen hundreds of Series A term sheets, works with founders to identify where terms diverge from standard, what can be done about divergences, and when negotiation makes sense.

YC drafted a standard, clean Series A term sheet representing what they see most often from good Silicon Valley VCs. The term sheet fits on a single page [1]. This brevity was not always the norm but became common over the last decade as some investors shortened their legalese as a signal: "We aren't going to get bogged down in the minutiae. We're going to make this easy, friendly, standard and fast" [1].

A [downloadable Word version](https://www.ycombinator.com/series_a_term_sheet/) of the standard term sheet is available from YC.

## Pricing

The lead investor in a Series A round generally wants 20% of the company, but pricing can flex depending on leverage held by each side. YC considers price important but too specific to each raise to standardize. Their focus is on control and structure terms that are less familiar to founders and more prone to cause confusion [1].

## Control Terms: What the Term Sheet Tells You

The term sheet is a signal about the investor. A contract allocates risks between parties, so the terms an investor insists on reveal their perceived risks [1].

### Board Composition

The standard term sheet is founder-friendly because founders retain board control at 2-1. The most common way founders lose control at Series A is through a 2-2-1 board structure: two founders, two investors, and one independent board member. Losing board control is significant because it means founders can be fired from their own company [1].

Another control risk is a separate provision requiring the investor director's approval for operational decisions such as setting annual budgets, hiring/firing executives, pivoting the business, or adding new lines of business. When investors structure boards to take power away from founders, the outward justification is governance or accountability. Kwon and Harris state plainly: "when an investor says that they're committed to partnering with you for the long-term -- or that they're betting everything on you -- but then tells you something else with the terms that they insist on, believe the terms" [1].

### Standard Investor Vetoes

The standard voting rights section includes vetoes on financing (clauses covering issuance of new equity) and sale of the company. Kwon and Harris note these are standard across most term sheets and use similar technical jargon, but their concrete implications are not facially obvious. Founders should have a lawyer walk through the scenarios [1].

Founders should ensure their lawyer and the investor's lawyer both specialize in startups and venture deals, where "standard" terms have accepted practice [1].

## Dirty Economic Terms to Watch For

The standard term sheet is instructive for what it does not contain. Non-standard or "dirty" economic terms include [1]:

- **Liquidation preference greater than 1x**: The investor gets back more than its invested capital first.
- **Participating preferred**: The investor double-dips by getting money back plus pro rata portion of exit proceeds, rather than choosing between the two.
- **Cumulative dividends**: The investor compounds its liquidation preference every year by X%, increasing the economic hurdle before founders and employees see value.
- **Warrant coverage**: The investor gets extra fully-diluted ownership without paying for it at the agreed valuation.

These are all ways of adding structure to reduce typical venture risk. The investor is "essentially saying, 'I'm sort of afraid of losing my money.'" It can foreshadow behavior when things go badly, such as pushing founders to sell when they do not want to, or pulling back on risk when risk is needed [1].

Good investors address economic risks by negotiating on valuation and are otherwise happy to give standard terms because "the real money in venture is not made with structure, but by building long-term value" [1].

## Series A Terms as Foundation

The Series A documents set a foundation and precedent for future rounds. Good foundations make the next term sheet and financing round fast and simple, as future investors step into the same straightforward terms. Bad foundations complicate everything: future investors ask for the same heavy terms, existing investors refuse to drop terms that new investors want removed. "Unwinding bad terms is difficult, and oftentimes impossible" [1].

## Don't Over-Optimize

The goal is a clean deal, not a perfect deal. "No one ever built an enduring company just by winning their Series A negotiation." Even if terms are not ideal, execution can outrun suboptimal terms or establish leverage to renegotiate later. The ultimate goal is closing fast and getting back to work [1].

## References

1. [A Standard and Clean Series A Term Sheet](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4P-a-standard-and-clean-series-a-term-sheet) — Jason Kwon, [[Aaron Harris]] (September 2018)

title: Co-Founders
body:
# Co-Founders

The co-founder relationship is one of the most consequential decisions in a startup's life. Of YC's [top companies](https://www.ycombinator.com/topcompanies), only 7.9% were founded by solo founders [3]. [[Paul Graham]] notes that exploitation of co-founders is often the first sign of a founder who will fail — the co-founder relationship is the foundation of the company [4].

## Why Co-Founders Matter

A co-founder provides three things: task distribution, emotional support, and complementary expertise [3]. One co-founder might be a talented programmer while the other excels at sales. Beyond functional roles, co-founders help bear the emotional weight of building a startup. Building a company from scratch is mentally and emotionally taxing, and it is usually too much for one person to manage [3].

## How Co-Founders Meet

Data from YC's top companies shows the most common ways founders connected [3]:

- School and work are the dominant channels
- Working on projects or extracurricular activities together is one of the best starting points
- College is described as "an ideal place to find a co-founder" [3]

Among women founders specifically, the distribution of how co-founders met shows similar patterns, though the data set (78 entries for women founders vs. 68 for top companies) reveals some differences in network composition [3].

## YC's Co-Founder Matching Platform

Y Combinator runs a [co-founder matching platform](https://www.ycombinator.com/cofounder-matching) where founders describe themselves and what they are looking for, and the system shows profiles that most closely match [1]. The platform has produced companies that were accepted into YC and raised significant funding.

### Sequin (YC S21)

Vrinda Gupta had left her job after launching credit cards like the Chase Sapphire Reserve at Visa. She spent a year as a solo founder building a product to help women build credit, raising a pre-seed and building an MVP. She needed a technical co-founder who was mission-driven and had fintech expertise [1].

She matched with Mark Thomas, who had ten years of engineering experience at PayPal, cared about gender equity, and had spent six years as CTO of family-oriented startups. Gupta recalls: "We met the day after we matched, and the day after, and then the day after that" [1]. They went through YC's Summer 2021 batch and raised $5.7M [1].

### Kiwi Biosciences (YC S21)

Anjie Liu started Kiwi Biosciences to solve her own problems with irritable bowel syndrome. She had a founding scientist for R&D but wanted a co-founder to help build a consumer brand and commercialize the product [1].

She found David Hachuel on the co-founder matching platform. Hachuel had previously sold a startup in the same space. They took a structured approach: both founders answered all 50 questions from [First Round's co-founder questionnaire](https://proof-assets.s3.amazonaws.com/firstround/50%20Questions%20for%20Co-Founders.pdf) and found deep alignment on "all the important things" — conflict resolution, vision for culture, and more [1]. They worked remotely for four months, met in person after getting into YC, and raised $1.5M [1].

## 10 Questions to Discuss with a Potential Co-Founder

YC compiled a list of the 10 most important questions to discuss before committing to work together, derived from talking to teams who met on co-founder matching and from analyzing the most common reasons founding teams break up [2]. The best time to go through these is after getting to know someone, finding an idea both are interested in, and before committing.

YC emphasizes that no questionnaire substitutes for spending a lot of time together in person. It usually takes several months to know if you are compatible [2].

A [Google Doc template](https://yc.link/10-questions-cf) is available. The recommended process: each founder copies the document, writes answers independently, then shares and discusses [2].

### The 10 Key Questions

1. **Why do you want to do this startup? What are your personal goals, financial and non-financial?** [2]
2. **Roles, titles, and division of responsibilities. Who will be CEO?** Roles change over time, but plan the first 6-12 months and decide on CEO [2].
3. **How will you split equity?** Resolve early. YC provides guidance in the [co-founder matching manual](https://yc.link/10-questions-cf-equity) [2].
4. **Where will the company be based? In-person or remote?** [2]
5. **What idea will you work on? Are you willing to pivot?** [2]
6. **What needs to happen for each person to go full-time?** Examples: raise $X from investors, validate with a paying customer, enjoy working together for two months [2].
7. **Personal financial situation.** Willingness to work without salary, how long savings last, minimum salary needed, whether either will invest money [2].
8. **Working schedule.** Days, hours, sustainability, non-work priorities [2].
9. **Team building philosophy.** In-person vs. remote, cultural values, approach to hiring and management [2].
10. **Conflict resolution. What happens if you decide not to work together anymore?** [2]

### Getting to Know Each Other

Beyond the 10 key questions, YC recommends additional questions including: how friends describe your strengths and weaknesses, what you are proud of accomplishing, free-time interests (successful co-founders consistently have overlapping outside-of-work interests), companies and founders you admire, and long-term life goals [2].

On stress responses, psychologist and founder coach Amy Buechler wrote about how attachment styles (anxious vs. avoidant) affect co-founder relationships [2].

### Checklist Before Committing

**Before deciding to work together** [2]:
- Meet in person and do a trial project
- Do reference calls on each other (transparently — agree on timing and swap suggested references)
- Have your significant other meet your potential co-founder

**After deciding to work together** [2]:
- Create a system to prioritize tasks and decide what to work on
- Set up a recurring weekly 1-on-1 ("Founder Syncs," a concept created by Amy Buechler) to discuss how the working relationship is going. Buechler wrote a [detailed guide](https://www.notion.so/The-Founder-Sync-54f855a12de749fa9e74e928ca7fa5a5) on running them [2]

## Solo Founders

While YC does fund solo founders, the data is not in their favor. Only 7.9% of YC top companies were solo-founded [3]. Roughly 10% of companies in each YC batch have a non-technical founder, but among YC's top companies, only three were founded by non-technical founders: Flexport, Snapdocs, and UpKeep [3].

## References

1. [Does Co-Founder Matching Work?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Cq-does-co-founder-matching-work) — Catheryn Li (2021)
2. [10 Questions to Discuss with a Potential Co-Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Hj-10-questions-to-discuss-with-a-potential-co-founder) — Y Combinator (2022)
3. [Startups for Students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/D0-startups-for-students) — Tamanna Khemani, Kat Mañalac (2021)
4. [Billionaires Build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build) — [[Paul Graham]] (December 2020)

title: Hard Tech Startups
body:
# Hard Tech Startups

Hard tech startups -- those building physical products, aerospace technology, medical devices, or other complex engineering -- face distinct challenges around demonstrating progress, raising capital, and navigating regulatory environments. Multiple YC founders and partners have shared lessons on how to make hard tech work within the accelerator model and beyond.

## Why Founders Matter in Hard Tech

[[Blake Scholl]], founder of [[Boom Technology]] (YC W16), argues that all the big breakthroughs in aviation came from founder-led companies. The first airplane came from bicycle entrepreneurs. The first practical airliner (DC-3) and the first jetliner (De Havilland Comet) were created by companies run by their founders. The last new commercial aircraft company was founded in 1921. The last founder retired from the industry in 1958 -- "which, coincidentally or not, was also the year we had the first jetliner" [1].

Since then, Boeing and Airbus have optimized: making existing designs more efficient and safer, but not improving human capability. Boeing's CEO publicly said their policy is "no more moonshots" -- from the company that was literally in the moonshot business. "I believe very strongly that if we want more human capability, we need entrepreneurs, we need founders, and we need new companies" [1].

Scholl identifies what founders bring that managers do not: "Founders have vision. Founders start things to make the world a better place. Founders take risks. They make bold bets" [1].

## Demonstrating Progress in Hardware

Hardware companies at YC must figure out what they can build and show at demo day. Software companies can ship an MVP relatively quickly; hardware has longer lead times. The product may not be done, in market, or even have a working demo [1].

Scholl's advice: "Figure out really early on something that both is and looks like actual, substantive progress towards the product" [1]. This means avoiding two traps:

1. **Meaningful but unappreciable progress**: Boom's simulation results hitting critical lift-drag ratios were technically significant but "no one's gonna understand it or believe it or get excited about it" [1].
2. **Progress that is just for show**: Building a flight simulator would have been impressive-looking but empty [1].

Boom's solution: they completed the high-level aerodynamic design, built a model of the airplane, 3D-printed a model of the jet engine, and physically brought to demo day one of the three actual engines that would power the first airplane. "People, of course, were like, 'OMG, jet engine.'" The key was that it was real -- they had found and purchased the engine they would fly [1].

### The Athelas Path: Hackathon to Shippable Product

Tanay Tandon describes how [[Athelas]] (YC S16) went from a proof-of-concept built overnight at YC Hacks 2014 to shipping medical devices to patients and hospitals [2].

The first version used a rubber piece and spherical magnifier attached to a smartphone camera with a blood sample held by a toilet paper roll -- similar in design to a [van Leeuwenhoek microscope](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antonie_van_Leeuwenhoek). "While functioning and a neat trick, someone needed to be physically holding the camera in place, the slide had to be moved around, with the lighting often being hard to fix. At the end of the day it was a nice experimental toy" [2].

The progression from hackathon prototype to shippable product required [2]:
- An automated blood processing mechanism for stained peripheral smears
- More robust computer vision for different cell types (deep learning plus traditional CV)
- Automated imaging without manual slide manipulation
- Clinical validation (350 patients tested against gold-standard Beckman Counter)
- FDA Class 2 510(k) submission

The path was slow because of college workload and rising hardware iteration costs. "Finals often meant days going by without any tangible progress whatsoever" [2]. During YC (summer batch, full-time), clinical validation was completed at the FEMAP family hospital, demonstrating high inter-rater agreement (100% 5-class inter-rater agreement) [2].

The device shipped at $250 per unit for point-of-care locations, homes, and clinical settings [2].

## Selling Before Building

Scholl's experience challenges the assumption that hard tech companies cannot sell before the product exists. YC's advice to Boom: "Get your ass out of stealth mode, it's not helping you" and "Go sell some airplanes" [1].

Scholl's reaction: "I don't know how 10 guys in a basement who say they're building supersonic jets are going to sell anything to airlines, but I could try. And it turns out you can" [1].

The group partners' mandate: "Show up on demo day with sales or your goose is pretty cooked." Scholl assessed his sales pipeline (United, Delta, Lufthansa, Air China) and concluded none could close in eight weeks. He focused instead on startup airlines and Virgin [1].

### The Virgin Deal

Boom pursued Richard Branson directly, ensuring two different avenues to him so neither could be blocked: friends inside Virgin Galactic, and astronaut Mark Kelly on Boom's advisory board. The team crashed a Virgin Galactic spaceship rollout to get 15 minutes with Branson over breakfast with his mother [1].

The critical ask: "Look, we're not asking for your money, we're asking, 'When this flies, do you want a Virgin logo on it?'" Scholl emphasizes: "Ask for the right thing. When you have a deal that's probably gonna be hard to close -- ask for what's really gonna help you" [1].

Using demo day as a deadline was equally critical. Virgin cared about its brand and publicity. "We are launching the company on demo day, and either you can be part of it or not" [1]. Virgin took demo day more seriously than expected.

The email from Virgin arrived 24 hours before Boom's stage slot: "You can announce the following -- We'll take the first 10 airplanes, and we've got options on them. And through Virgin Galactic, we're gonna help you build it" [1].

Boom went from potential embarrassment to a record-setting $5 billion in LOIs at demo day [1].

### Sales Lessons for Hard Tech

Scholl distills three principles:
1. **Be persistent beyond comfort**: "Be so persistent that you're sure you're being annoying. And you'll feel like you must be annoying, but when you have that feeling that means you're probably doing it right" [1].
2. **Have a deadline**: demo day provided a believable, external forcing function [1].
3. **Make the deal sweet for the other side**: when in a hurry, maximize the value proposition for the counterparty [1].

## The Motivation Advantage of Big Ideas

Scholl argues that bigger ideas can actually be easier because they motivate the founder, attract better talent, and energize everyone involved. "The team we have around us at Boom is phenomenal, and we wouldn't have it if we were building an app or a small airplane, even. We're able to get these people because everyone looks at what we're building, and they're like, 'I can tell my grandkids about this! It's either we're going to fail or we're going to change the world. There's no in between'" [1].

His advice for choosing what to work on: "Find something in the world that you desperately want to exist." All startups go through hell; the question is whether it is worth it. "If you haven't found that, don't start a startup just to start a startup. I did that my first time and it was a horrible idea" [1].

## Regulatory and Validation Challenges

Hard tech startups often face regulatory hurdles that software companies do not. Athelas needed FDA approval (Class 2 510(k)) to ship its blood diagnostics device [2]. [[Jared Friedman]] notes that for hardtech and biotech companies, [[Series A Fundraising]] traction sections should focus on technology efficacy, patents, LOIs, and specific regulatory milestones like 510(k) or IND filings [3].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling Aerospace Company Boom](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5Q-on-starting-and-scaling-aerospace-company-boom) -- [[Blake Scholl]]
2. [On Starting a Company from a Hackathon Project](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project) -- Tanay Tandon
3. [How to Spin Your Scientific Research Out of a University and Into a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8f-how-to-spin-your-scientific-research-out-of-a-university-and-into-a-startup) -- [[Jared Friedman]]

title: Series A Fundraising
body:
# Series A Fundraising

The Series A is the first major priced equity round for most startups. It involves two distinct challenges: building and delivering a compelling pitch, and navigating the legal and organizational complexity of closing. This article covers both, drawing on Janelle Tam's guide to Series A pitches and [[Aaron Harris]] and Jason Kwon's diligence checklist.

## The Series A Pitch

Janelle Tam, YC's Series A Program Manager, compiled feedback from hundreds of hours working 1:1 with founders on their Series A pitches and decks. Every piece of advice in her guide was given to at least one YC founder internally [2].

### Purpose and Principles

The purpose is to show investors why they should invest. The pitch provides a clear and concise overview of the business already built, then paints a picture of where it could go and why raising money will help get there [2].

Six principles underlie this:

1. **It is an investor pitch, not a customer pitch.** Investors may not have encountered the problem being solved. Good pitches explain the problem and its impact, show how the product solves it, and demonstrate why it is a good business [2].
2. **Clarity is paramount.** Investor attention is a limited resource. Make the pitch as simple and straightforward as possible. The best pitchers use plain English, avoiding jargon. Where jargon is necessary, they define each term clearly on first use [2].
3. **Give an overview, not a download.** The goal is to tantalize investors with enough information to keep them interested, leaving room for questions. A pitch should be 15-20 minutes, setting up a 40-45 minute discussion. If investors are not asking questions, the slides are probably too complicated [2].
4. **Start with what you have built.** Compared to the seed round, it is harder to raise a Series A on promise alone. Investors want to see results [2].
5. **Paint a picture of where it is going.** Once you establish credibility through what you have already built, show how it could grow into a massive company [2].
6. **Show that money is the obstacle.** The deck should make clear that capital -- not incompetence, poor product quality, or lack of [[Product-Market Fit]] -- is what stands between the company and the next level [2].

### The Starting Exercise

Tam describes a structured process for building the pitch [2]:

1. Write the narrative as 10-15 bullet points -- the "fundraising vertebrae"
2. Under each bullet, list supporting data points
3. Build the deck around this list: each bullet is one slide. If more than one point per slide, the slide is too complicated. The deck should be 10-15 slides plus appendix
4. Write out every question investors could ask, especially about metrics, and prepare clear, credible, data-backed answers

### Deck Structure

**Title (1 slide):** Company name or logo and a one-sentence description. The one-liner must be concrete ("We build and operate robot greenhouses" not "Reinventing agriculture"), understandable to a layperson, and specific to what the company does [2].

**Traction Teaser (1 slide):** Placed early to grab attention and convince the investor the presentation is worth listening to [2].

**Problem (1 slide):** The problem must match the solution. Present from the customer's perspective. Show, don't tell -- "Medical billing is broken" is vague; "Here's how medical billing works today: patients need to do X steps, which takes Y months and results in Z% claims approved" is concrete [2].

**Solution (1 slide):** Side-by-side comparison with status quo. Quantify impact with numbers. Focus on what exists now, not future plans. Show how the customer uses it, not feature lists [2].

**Traction In-Depth (a few slides):** Numbers should tell a story, starting with the most important metric -- usually revenue. For enterprise: revenue growth, sales cycle, ACV, salesperson efficiency, churn. For consumer: revenue plus growth rate, organic vs. paid acquisition, CAC/LTV, engagement and retention.
